1	The goals of this section are to revisit the Betti map, the Betti form and make a link between them. 
2	In this paper we construct the Betti map using the universal family of
3	principally polarized abelian varieties with level-$\ell$-structure and
4	bypass the ad-hoc construction found in \cite{GaoHab}.
5	
6	 In this section we will make
7	the following assumptions.
8	All varieties  are defined over the field $\IC$.
9	Let $S$ be an  irreducible, regular, quasi-projective variety over $\IC$. 
10	Let $\pi\colon \cA\rightarrow S$ be an abelian scheme of  relative
11	dimension $g$, that carries a principal polarization, and such that
12	 $\cA$ is equipped with level-$\ell$-structure, for some
13	 $\ell\ge\bestlevel$, \textit{i.e.}, {\tt (Hyp)} is satisfied. 
14	
15	\begin{proposition}\label{PropBettiMap}
16	Let $s_0 \in S(\IC)$. Then there exist an open neighborhood $\Delta$ of $s_0$ in $S^{\mathrm{an}}$, and a map $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} := \pi^{-1}(\Delta) \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}$, called the Betti map, with the following properties.
17	\begin{enumerate}
18	\item[(i)] For each $s \in \Delta$ the restriction $b_{\Delta}|_{\cA_s(\IC)} \colon \cA_s(\IC) \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}$ is a group isomorphism.
19	\item[(ii)] For each $\xi \in \mathbb T^{2g}$ the preimage $b_{\Delta}^{-1}(\xi)$ is a complex analytic subset of $\cA_{\Delta}$.
20	\item[(iii)] The product
21	$(b_{\Delta},\pi) \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb
22	T^{2g} \times \Delta$ is a real analytic isomorphism.% 
23	\end{enumerate}
24	\end{proposition}
25	
26	The properties (i) -- (iii) do not uniquely determine $b_\Delta$.
27	Indeed,  composing $b_\Delta$ with an automorphism of the
28	topological group $\IT^{2g}$, \textit{i.e.}, an element of
29	$\mathrm{GL}_{2g}(\IZ)$, leads to a new Betti map satisfying (i) --
30	(iii). After shrinking   $\Delta$ we may assume that it is connected.
31	In this case, an application of the
32	Baire Category Theorem shows that $b_\Delta$ is
33	uniquely determined by (i) and (iii)
34	up to composition with a unique element of $\mathrm{GL}_{2g}(\IZ)$.
35	
36	
37	Andr\'{e}, Corvaja, and Zannier~\cite{ACZBetti} recently began the
38	study of the maximal rank of the Betti map, especially the submersivity, using a slightly different
39	definition. A full study of this maximal rank was realized in \cite{GaoBettiRank}. Closely related to the
40	Betti map is the Betti form, a semi-positive $(1,1)$-form on
41	$\an{\cA}$, which was first introduced in Mok \cite{Mok11Form}.
42	
43	\begin{proposition}\label{PropBettiForm}
44	There exists a closed $(1,1)$-form $\omega$ on $\an{\cA}$, called the Betti form, such that the following properties hold.
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	\item[(i)] The $(1,1)$-form $\omega$ is semi-positive, \textit{i.e.},  at each point
47	the associated Hermitian form is positive semi-definite.
48	\item[(ii)] For all $N\in\IZ$ we have $[N]^*\omega = N^2 \omega$.
49	\item[(iii)] If $X$ is an irreducible subvariety of $\cA$ of dimension
50	$d$ and $\Delta\subset \an{S}$ is open with $\sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta\not=\emptyset$, then
51	\[
52	\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d} \not\equiv 0 \quad\text{if and only
53	if}\quad  \max_{x \in
54	\sman{X} \cap \cA_{\Delta}} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR}
55	(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{X^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x = 2d. %\text{ for some non-empty open }\Delta\subseteq S^{\mathrm{an}}.
56	\]
57	
58	\end{enumerate}
59	\end{proposition}
60	
61	
62	We will prove both propositions during the course of this section using the universal abelian variety. A dynamical approach can be found in \cite[$\mathsection$2]{CGHX:18}.
63	
64	\subsection{Betti map for the universal abelian variety}\label{SubsectionBettiMap}
65	Our proof of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap} follows the construction in \cite[$\mathsection$3-$\mathsection$4]{GaoBettiRank}. We divide it into several steps.
66	
67	We start to prove Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap} for $S = \mathbb
68	A_g$, the moduli space of principally polarized abelian variety of
69	dimension $g$ with level-$\ell$-structure;  it is a fine moduli
70	space. Let $\pi^{\mathrm{univ}} \colon \mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$ be the universal abelian variety.
71	
72	The universal covering $\mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \an{\mathbb A}_g$,
73	where $\mathfrak{H}_g$ is the Siegel upper half space, gives a
74	polarized family of abelian varieties
75	$\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g} \rightarrow \mathfrak{H}_g$ fitting into the diagram
76	\[
77	\xymatrix{
78	\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g} := \mathfrak{A}_g \times_{\an{\mathbb A}_g}\mathfrak{H}_g \ar[r]^-{u_B} \ar[d] & \an{\mathfrak{A}}_g \ar[d]^{\pi^{\mathrm{univ}}} \\
79	\mathfrak{H}_g \ar[r] & \an{\mathbb A}_g.
80	}
81	\]
82	For the universal covering $u \colon \IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g
83	\rightarrow \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g}$ and for each $Z \in
84	\mathfrak{H}_g$, the kernel of $u|_{\mathbb{C}^g \times \{Z\}}$ is
85	$\mathbb{Z}^g + Z \mathbb{Z}^g$. Thus the map $\IC^g \times
86	\mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \IR^g \times \IR^g \times
87	\mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \IR^{2g}$, where the first map is the
88	inverse of $(a,b,Z) \mapsto (a + Z  b,Z)$ and the second map is the
89	natural projection, descends to a \textit{real} analytic map
90	\[
91	b^{\mathrm{univ}} \colon \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}.
92	\]
93	 Now for each $s_0 \in \mathbb A_g(\IC)$, there exists a contractible,
94	 relatively compact, open neighborhood $\Delta$ of $s_0$ in $\mathbb A_g^{\mathrm{an}}$ such that $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\Delta}:=(\pi^{\mathrm{univ}})^{-1}(\Delta)$ can be identified with $\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,\Delta'}$ for some open subset $\Delta'$ of $\mathfrak{H}_g$. The composite $b_{\Delta} \colon \mathfrak{A}_{g,\Delta} \cong \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,\Delta'} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}$ is  real analytic and satisfies the three properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap}. Thus $b_{\Delta}$ is the desired Betti map in this case. Note that for a fixed (small enough) $\Delta$, there are infinitely choices of $\Delta'$; but for $\Delta$ small enough, if $\Delta_1'$ and $\Delta_2'$ are two such choices, then $\Delta_2' = \alpha \cdot \Delta_1'$ for some $\alpha \in \mathrm{Sp}_{2g}(\IZ) \subset \mathrm{SL}_{2g}(\IZ)$. Thus we have proved Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap} for $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$.
95	
96	
97	\subsection{Betti form for the universal abelian variety}
98	
99	\newcommand{\X}{X}
100	\newcommand{\Y}{Y}
101	\newcommand{\rmd}{\mathrm{d}}
102	\newcommand{\tran}[1]{{{#1}}^{\!^{\intercal}}}
103	
104	
105	For the universal covering $\mathbf{u} = u_B\circ u \colon \IC^g
106	\times \mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g^{\mathrm{an}}$, we
107	will use $(w,Z)$ to denote the coordinates on $\IC^g \times
108	\mathfrak{H}_g$.
109	Below $\mathrm{Im}$ denotes imaginary part. 
110	%% We will decompose an element $Z\in \mathfrak{H}_g$  as $Z = \X
111	%% + \sqrt{-1}\Y$
112	%% with $\X$ the real part and $\Y$ the imaginary part of $Z$. 
113	
114	
115	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaBettiOnUniversalCovering}
116	%Under the change of coordinates \eqref{EqChangeOfCoor}, the $2$-form $2\mathrm{d}a \wedge \mathrm{d} b$ becomes
117	Define
118	\[
119	\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}:=\sqrt{-1}\partial \overline{\partial} \left( 2 (\mathrm{Im}w)^{\!^{\intercal}} (\mathrm{Im} Z)^{-1} (\mathrm{Im}w) \right).
120	\]
121	Then $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ is a closed  semi-positive
122	$(1,1)$-form on $\IC^g\times\mathfrak{H}_g$ satisfying %we have
123	\begin{equation}
124	  \label{eq:bettiformaltformula}
125	  \hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}} = \sqrt{-1} \tran{\left( \rmd Z \Y^{-1}
126	      \mathrm{Im}(w) - \rmd w\right)} \wedge \Y^{-1} \left( \rmd \overline Z \Y^{-1}
127	    \mathrm{Im}(w) - \rmd \overline w\right)
128	\end{equation}
129	with $\Y = \mathrm{Im}(Z)$; here and below the symbol $\wedge$
130	is used as a combination of wedge product and matrix multiplication
131	when appropriate. %\Pcomment{New comment, referee .3}
132	Moreover, if $N\in\IZ$ and if we denote by $\widetilde{N} \colon \IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g$ the map $(w,Z) \mapsto (Nw,Z)$, then $\widetilde{N}^*\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}} = N^2\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$.
133	\end{lemma}
134	\begin{proof}
135	The $(1,1)$-form $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ is  closed since $\mathrm{d} = \partial + \overline{\partial}$. We will prove the semi-positivity by direct computation.
136	
137	% Using \Pinlinecomment{TODO: Referee .3 wants explanation for formulas
138	%   on right of next two lines.}
139	%   \footnote{It is worth explaining the formulae on the right. We hereby do it for $\partial \Y^{-1} = \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2} \Y^{-1} \mathrm{d}  Z \Y^{-1}$ and the other one is similar. Taking partial derivatives on both sides of $\Y \Y^{-1} = I$, we get $(\partial \Y)\Y^{-1} + \Y \partial Y^{-1} = 0$. So $\partial \Y^{-1} = - \Y^{-1}(\partial \Y)\Y^{-1}$. But $\partial \Y = \partial \mathrm{Im}Z = - \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2}\mathrm{d} Z$. Hence we get the desired formula for $\partial \Y^{-1}$.}
140	   
141	We have the following formulae for partial derivatives
142	  \begin{equation*}    
143	%    \label{eq:dwdZ}
144	    \begin{aligned}
145	      \overline\partial \mathrm{Im} w &=& \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2} \mathrm{d}\overline w, \qquad     
146	      \overline\partial (\Y^{-1}) &=&& -\frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2} \Y^{-1} \mathrm{d} \overline Z
147	      \Y^{-1},\\
148	      \partial \mathrm{Im} w &=& -\frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2} \mathrm{d} w,\qquad
149	      \partial (\Y^{-1}) &=&& \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2} \Y^{-1} \mathrm{d}  Z \Y^{-1}.
150	    \end{aligned}
151	  \end{equation*}
152	Let us prove the formulae on the right. We hereby do it for $\partial (\Y^{-1}) = \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2} \Y^{-1} \mathrm{d}  Z \Y^{-1}$ and the other one is similar. Taking partial derivatives on both sides of $\Y \Y^{-1} = I$, we get $(\partial \Y)\Y^{-1} + \Y \partial (Y^{-1}) = 0$. So $\partial (\Y^{-1}) = - \Y^{-1}(\partial \Y)\Y^{-1}$. But $\partial \Y = \partial \mathrm{Im}Z = - \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2}\mathrm{d} Z$. Hence we get the desired formula for $\partial (\Y^{-1})$.
153	  
154	Using these formulae  
155	and the Leibniz rule (note that $Z = Z^{\!^{\intercal}}$ and hence $\mathrm{d}Z = \mathrm{d}Z^{\!^{\intercal}}$), we get
156	\begin{align*}
157	    \hat\omega^{\mathrm{univ}} = \sqrt{-1} \big( &
158	 (\mathrm{d}w)^{\!^{\intercal}} \Y^{-1} \wedge  \mathrm{d}\overline{w} + (\mathrm{Im}w)^{\!^{\intercal}} \Y^{-1}  \mathrm{d}Z \wedge \Y^{-1}  \mathrm{d}\overline{Z}  \Y^{-1} (\mathrm{Im}w) \\
159	 & - (\mathrm{Im}w)^{\!^{\intercal}} \Y^{-1} \mathrm{d}Z
160	 \Y^{-1} \wedge  \mathrm{d}\overline{w} -
161	 (\mathrm{d}w)^{\!^{\intercal}}   \wedge \Y^{-1}  \mathrm{d}\overline{Z} \Y^{-1} (\mathrm{Im}w) \big). 
162	\end{align*}
163	  Rearranging yields the desired equality (\ref{eq:bettiformaltformula}). 
164	  The associated  form is 
165	  \begin{equation*}
166	    H:\bigl((\xi_w,\xi_Z),(\eta_w,\eta_Z)\bigr) \mapsto 
167	    \tran{\left( \xi_Z \Y^{-1}
168	        \mathrm{Im}(w) - \xi_w\right)}  \Y^{-1} \left( \overline{\eta_Z} \Y^{-1}
169	      \mathrm{Im}(w) -  \overline{\eta_w}\right),
170	  \end{equation*}
171	  for $\xi_w,\eta_w \in \IC^g$ and $\xi_Z,\eta_Z\in
172	  \mathrm{Mat}_g(\IC)$ symmetric, is Hermitian and so
173	  $\hat \omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ is real. Moreover, 
174	    \begin{equation*}
175	    H\bigl((\xi_w,\xi_Z),(\xi_w,\xi_Z)\bigr) = \tran{v} \Y^{-1} \overline v
176	    \quad\text{with}\quad v =  \xi_Z \Y^{-1}
177	        \mathrm{Im}(w) - \xi_w.
178	  \end{equation*}
179	  But $\Y$ is positive definite as a real symmetric matrix and thus
180	  positive definite as a Hermitian matrix. We see that $H$
181	  is positive semi-definite and this implies that
182	  $\hat\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ is positive semi-definite.
183	
184	  The ``moreover'' part of the lemma is clear.    
185	\end{proof}
186	
187	Next we want to show that $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ descends to a $(1,1)$-form on $\an{\mathfrak{A}}_g$. To do this, we first show that $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ can be written in a simple form under an appropriate change of coordinates.
188	
189	Define the complex space $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$, which is the universal covering of $\an{\mathfrak{A}}_g$, as follows:
190	\begin{itemize}
191	\item As a real algebraic space, $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}} := \mathbb R^{2g} \times \mathfrak{H}_g$.
192	\item The complex structure on $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$ is given by
193	\begin{equation}\label{EqChangeOfCoor}
194	\mathbb R^{2g} \times \mathfrak{H}_g = \mathbb R^g \times \mathbb R^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \cong \mathbb C^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g, \qquad (a,b,Z) \mapsto (a+Zb, Z).
195	\end{equation}
196	\end{itemize}
197	
198	
199	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaBettiFormComplexReal}
200	Let $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ be as in
201	Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiOnUniversalCovering}. Then under the change of
202	coordinates \eqref{EqChangeOfCoor}, we have 
203	$\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}=2\tran{(\mathrm{d}a)} \wedge \mathrm{d}
204	b$.
205	\end{lemma}
206	\begin{proof} For the moment we write $Z = \X + \sqrt{-1}\Y$ with $\X$ and $\Y$ the real and
207	imaginary part of $Z\in\mathfrak{H}_g$, respectively. 
208	Note that $w = a + Zb = (a + \X b) + \sqrt{-1}\Y b$. Hence
209	$\Y^{-1}(\mathrm{Im}w) = b$ and $\mathrm{d}w = \mathrm{d}a +
210	Z\mathrm{d}b + \mathrm{d}Z  b$. Using this and noting that $Z$
211	is symmetric, we have that \eqref{eq:bettiformaltformula} becomes
212	\begin{equation*}
213	%\label{EqFirstTerm11Form}
214	\begin{aligned}
215	    \hat \omega^{\mathrm{univ}} &=\sqrt{-1}\left( \sqrt{-1}\tran{(\rmd
216	        b)} \Y + \tran{(\rmd b)} \X + \tran{(\rmd a)}\right) \wedge \Y^{-1}
217	    \left(\rmd a + \X \rmd b - \sqrt{-1} \Y \rmd b\right) \\
218	    &=\sqrt{-1}\bigl(\sqrt{-1} \tran{(\rmd b)}\wedge\rmd a + \tran{(\rmd b)}\wedge
219	    \Y \rmd b + \tran{(\rmd b)} \X \wedge \Y^{-1} \rmd a+ \tran{(\rmd
220	      b)}\X\wedge 
221	    \Y^{-1}\X \rmd b + \\ 
222	    &\phantom{=\sqrt{-1}\bigl(}\tran{(\rmd a)} \wedge \Y^{-1} \rmd a 
223	    + \tran{(\rmd a)}\wedge \Y^{-1}\X\rmd b -
224	    \sqrt{-1} \tran{(\rmd a)}\wedge \rmd b\bigr).
225	\end{aligned}
226	\end{equation*}
227	
228	 Many terms will vanish. Indeed, if $M$ is a
229	matrix, then $\tran{(\rmd b)} \wedge M \rmd a = - \tran{(\rmd
230	a)}\wedge \tran{M} \rmd b $. As $\tran{(\X\Y^{-1})} = \Y^{-1}\X$ and as
231	$\tran{(\rmd b)} \X \wedge \Y^{-1} \rmd a=\tran{(\rmd
232	b)} \wedge \X\Y^{-1} \rmd a$ we find
233	$\tran{(\rmd b)}\X \wedge \Y^{-1} \rmd a + \tran{(\rmd a)}\wedge
234	\Y^{-1}\X \rmd b=0$. Observe that $\Y$ is symmetric, and so $\tran{(\rmd
235	b)}\wedge \Y \rmd b = -\tran{(\rmd b)}\wedge \Y\rmd b$ vanishes. Arguing
236	along the same line and using that
237	$\Y^{-1}$ and $\X\Y^{-1}\X$ are  symmetric we find $\tran{(\rmd
238	a)}\wedge \Y^{-1} \rmd a = 0$ and $\tran{(\rmd b)}\X\wedge \Y^{-1}\X=\tran{(\rmd b)}\wedge \X\Y^{-1}\X \rmd
239	b=0$. We are left with
240	$    \hat \omega^{\mathrm{univ}} = 2\tran{(\rmd a)} \wedge \rmd b$. 
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	
244	\begin{corollary}\label{CorBettiFormVanishingDirection}
245	  Let $\hat{C}$ be an irreducible, $1$-dimensional, complex analytic
246	  subset of an open subset of $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}
247	  = \IR^{2g} \times \mathfrak{H}_g$ and $\sm{\hat {C}}$ its smooth
248	  locus. Then $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ restricted to $\sm{\hat C}$
249	  is trivial if and only if
250	  $\hat{C} \subseteq \{r\} \times \mathfrak{H}_g$ for some
251	  $r \in \mathbb R^{2g}$.
252	\end{corollary}
253	\begin{proof}
254	  First, assume that the coordinates $(a,b)$ of $\IR^{2g}$ are constant on
255	  $\hat C$. Then $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$, which is simply
256	  $2 \tran{(\rmd a)} \wedge \mathrm{d}b$ by
257	  Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiFormComplexReal}, vanishes on $\sm{\hat C}$.
258	
259	  Conversely, suppose that $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ vanishes
260	  identically on $\sm{\hat C}$. This time we use (\ref{eq:bettiformaltformula}) from 
261	  Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiOnUniversalCovering}. As $\Y^{-1}$ is positive
262	  definite we find $\rmd Z \Y^{-1} \mathrm{Im}(w) = \rmd w$ on $\sm{\hat C}$.
263	  Using the change of coordinates $w = a+Zb$ we deduce $\mathrm{Im}(w) = \Y b$
264	  and $\rmd w = \rmd a
265	  + \rmd Z b +Z\rmd b$. So $\rmd Z b  =\rmd Z \Y^{-1} \mathrm{Im}(w)
266	  = \rmd w=  \rmd a
267	  + \rmd Z b + Z\rmd b$ on $\sm{\hat C}$. This equality simplifies to
268	  $\rmd a + Z \rmd b = 0$ on $\sm{\hat C}$. As $a$ and $b$ are real
269	  valued and as $Z\in \mathfrak{H}_g$ we conclude $\rmd a = \rmd b =0$
270	  on $\sm{\hat C}$. So $a$ and $b$ are constant on $\hat C$.
271	\end{proof}
272	
273	
274	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaBettiFormUnivAb}
275	Let $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ be as in
276	Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiOnUniversalCovering}. Then
277	$\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ descends to a  semi-positive
278	$(1,1)$-form $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ on $\mathfrak{A}_g$. Moreover,
279	for $N\in\IZ$  we have $[N]^*\omega^{\mathrm{univ}} = N^2 \omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$.
280	\end{lemma}
281	\begin{proof}
282	Let $\mathrm{Sp}_{2g}$ be the symplectic group defined over $\mathbb Q$, and let $V_{2g}$ be the vector group over $\mathbb Q$ of dimension $2g$. Then the natural action of $\mathrm{Sp}_{2g}$ on $V_{2g}$ defines a group $P_{2g,\mathrm{a}}:= V_{2g} \rtimes \mathrm{Sp}_{2g}$.
283	
284	We use the classical action of $\mathrm{Sp}_{2g}(\mathbb R)$  on
285	$\mathfrak{H}_g$, it is transitive. The real coordinate on $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$ on the left hand side of \eqref{EqChangeOfCoor} has the following advantage. The group $P_{2g,\mathrm{a}}(\mathbb R)$ acts transitively on $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$ by the formula
286	\[
287	(v,h) \cdot (v',Z) := (v+hv',hZ)
288	\]
289	for $(v,h) \in P_{2g,\mathrm{a}}(\mathbb R)$ and $(v',Z) \in \mathbb R^{2g} \times \mathfrak{H}_g = \mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$. The space $\mathfrak{A}_g^{\mathrm{an}}$ is then obtained as the quotient of $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$ by a congruence subgroup of $P_{2g,\mathrm{a}}(\mathbb Q)$. We refer to \cite[10.5--10.9]{PinkThesis} or \cite[Construction~2.9 and Example~2.12]{Pink05} for these facts.
290	
291	%We thus obtain the universal covering $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}} \rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g$.
292	
293	It is clear that both $V_{2g}(\mathbb R)$ and
294	$\mathrm{Sp}_{2g}(\mathbb R)$ preserve $2\tran{(\rmd a)} \wedge \mathrm{d} b$. Thus this $2$-form is invariant under the action of $P_{2g,\mathrm{a}}(\mathbb R)$ on $\mathcal X_{2g,\mathrm{a}}$.
295	
296	So by Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiFormComplexReal}, the previous two paragraphs imply that $\hat{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}$ descends to a $(1,1)$-form $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ on $\mathfrak{A}_g$. The semi-positivity of $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ follows from Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiOnUniversalCovering}.
297	
298	The property $[N]^*\omega^{\mathrm{univ}} = N^2 \omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ follows from the ``moreover'' part of Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiOnUniversalCovering} and the following commutative diagram
299	\[
300	\begin{gathered}[b]
301	\xymatrix{
302	\IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \ar[r]^-{\widetilde{N}} \ar[d] & \IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \ar[d] \\
303	\an{\mathfrak{A}}_g \ar[r]^-{[N]} & \an{\mathfrak{A}}_g.
304	}\\[-\dp\strutbox]
305	\end{gathered}
306	\qedhere
307	\]
308	\end{proof}
309	
310	
311	%Thus Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm} for $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$ follows from Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiFormUnivAb} and Corollary~\ref{CorBettiFormVanishingDirection}.
312	
313	This semi-positive $(1,1)$-form $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ will be the Betti form for $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$, as desired in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}. To show this, it suffices to establish property (iii) of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}. Hence it suffices to prove the following proposition. %% Here we use the notations in $\mathsection$\ref{SubsectionBettiMap}: recall the map $b^{\mathrm{univ}} \colon \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ in this case and the natural projection $u_B \colon \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g} = \mathfrak{A}_g \times_{\mathbb A_g} \mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g$.
314	
315	\begin{proposition}\label{PropBettiFormNonDegUnivAb} 
316	Assume $\cA \rightarrow S$ is $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$. Let $X$ be an irreducible subvariety of $\mathfrak{A}_g$ of dimension
317	$d$ and let $\Delta$ be an open subset of $\an{S}$ with $\sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta\not=\emptyset$. Then
318	\begin{equation}
319	\label{prop:bettformvsrankuniv}
320	 \omega^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d} \not\equiv 0
321	\quad\text{if and only if}\quad
322	\max_{x \in \sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_\Delta|_{\sman{X}})_{x} = 2d.
323	\end{equation}
324	%where $\widetilde{X}$ is a complex analytic irreducible component of $u_B^{-1}(X^{\mathrm{sm}})$.
325	\end{proposition}
326	\begin{proof}
327	%% Denote by $\widetilde{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}} := u_B^*(\omega^{\mathrm{univ}})$. 
328	%% It is clear that
329	%% \[
330	%% \omega^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{X^{\mathrm{sm}}}^{\wedge d} \not\equiv 0 \Leftrightarrow \widetilde{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\widetilde{X}}^{\wedge d} \not\equiv 0.
331	%% \]
332	%% Thus it suffices to establish the following property. For each $\widetilde{x} \in \widetilde{X}$, we have $(\widetilde{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\widetilde{X}}^{\wedge d})_{\widetilde{x}} = 0$ if and only if
333	%% \begin{equation}\label{EqRankDegeneracy}
334	%% \mathrm{rank}_{\mathbb R}(\mathrm{d}b^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\widetilde{X}})_{\widetilde{x}} < 2d.
335	%% \end{equation}
336	We begin by  reformulating
337	Corollary~\ref{CorBettiFormVanishingDirection}.
338	If ${C}$ is an irreducible, $1$-dimensional, complex analytic subset
339	of an open subset of ${\cA_\Delta}$, then
340	\begin{equation}\label{EqBettiFormVanishingDirection}
341	{\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sm{C}} = 0 \quad\text{if
342	and only if}\quad b_\Delta({C})\text{ is a point};
343	\end{equation}
344	indeed, this claim is local and it follows using the universal
345	covering
346	$\mathbf{u} \colon \IC^g\times\mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g$. 
347	
348	We assume first that the right side of (\ref{prop:bettformvsrankuniv})
349	is false, \textit{i.e.}, the maximal rank is strictly less than
350	$2d=2\dim X$.
351	So  every $x\in \sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta$  is a non-isolated point of  
352	$b_\Delta^{-1}(r) \cap \sman{X}$  where $r =
353	b_\Delta(x)$. Because 
354	$b_\Delta^{-1}(r)$ is a complex analytic subset of $\cA_\Delta$ (by Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap}.(ii) for $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$) and $\sman{X}$ is complex
355	analytic in a neighborhood of $x$ in $\an{\cA}$, there exists  an irreducible
356	complex analytic curve ${C}$ in  $b_\Delta^{-1}(r) \cap \sman{X}$ passing
357	through $x$. In
358	particular, $b_\Delta({C})$ is a point
359	%and $\widetilde{x} \in \widetilde{C} \subseteq \widetilde{X}$,
360	and so ${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sm{C}} \equiv 0$ by \eqref{EqBettiFormVanishingDirection}.
361	
362	The upshot of the previous paragraph is that the Hermitian form
363	attached to the semi-positive $(1,1)$-form ${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{X}}$
364	 vanishes along the tangent space of $\sm{C}$; it is degenerate.
365	We can complete a tangent vector of $\sm{C}$  to a basis of
366	the tangent space of $\sman{X}$. Considering holomorphic local
367	coordinates
368	we find ${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}=0$ at every
369	point of $\sm{C}$. By continuity it also vanishes at $x \in C$. Since
370	$x\in \sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta$ was arbitrary, we conclude
371	${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}\equiv 0$. 
372	
373	For the converse we assume
374	${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}\equiv 0$. So the
375	Hermitian form attached to this semi-positive $(1,1)$-form is
376	degenerate. Thus for each $x \in \sman{X}$, using holomorphic local coordinates we find an irreducible,  $1$-dimensional,  complex analytic subset $C_x$ which passes through $x$ and is contained in $\sman{X}$ such that $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{X}}$ vanishes along the tangent space of $\sm{C}_x$. So ${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sm{C}_x}\equiv 0$, and hence 
377	%Using holomorphic local coordinates 
378	% any prescribed auxiliary point of $\sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta$ lies in an 
379	%irreducible, $1$-dimensional,  complex analytic subset $C$ of an open
380	%subset of  $\sman{X}$ with
381	%${\omega}^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sm{C}}\equiv 0$. So
382	 $b_\Delta(C_x)$ is a point
383	by \eqref{EqBettiFormVanishingDirection}.
384	Letting the point $x$ run over $\sman{X}$, we conclude that the rank on the right
385	side of (\ref{prop:bettformvsrankuniv}) is strictly less than $2d$. 
386	\end{proof}

389	\subsection{General case}
390	We now prove Propositions~\ref{PropBettiMap}
391	and \ref{PropBettiForm} for
392	 $\pi \colon \cA \rightarrow S$ as near the beginning of this section.
393	 In particular, we assume \texttt{(Hyp)}. With the construction in $\mathsection$\ref{SubsectionBettiMap}, the rest of the proof of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap} follows the construction in \cite[$\mathsection$4]{GaoBettiRank}. 
394	
395	%% By assumption, $\cA$  carries a principal polarization
396	%% and  level-$\ell$-structure.
397	
398	As
399	$\IA_g$ is a fine moduli space there exists a Cartesian diagram
400	\begin{equation*}%\label{EqEmbedAbSchIntoUnivAbVar}
401	\xymatrix{
402	\cA \ar[r]^-{\iota} \ar[d]_{\pi} \pullbackcorner & \mathfrak{A}_g \ar[d] \\
403	S \ar[r]^-{\iota_S} & \mathbb{A}_g.
404	}
405	\end{equation*}
406	
407	Now let $s_0 \in S(\IC)$. Applying Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap} to the universal abelian variety $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$ and $\iota_S(s_0) \in \mathbb A_g(\IC)$, we obtain an open neighborhood $\Delta_0$ of $\iota_S(s_0)$ in $\mathbb A_g^{\mathrm{an}}$ and a map
408	\[
409	b_{\Delta_0} \colon \mathfrak{A}_g|_{\Delta_0} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}
410	\]
411	satisfying the properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap}.
412	
413	Now let $\Delta = \iota_S^{-1}(\Delta_0)$. Then $\Delta$ is an open neighborhood of $s$ in $S^{\mathrm{an}}$. Denote by $\cA_{\Delta} = \pi^{-1}(\Delta)$ and define
414	\[
415	b_{\Delta} = b_{\Delta_0} \circ \iota \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}.
416	\]
417	Then $b_{\Delta}$ satisfies the properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap} for $\cA \rightarrow S$. Hence $b_{\Delta}$ is our desired Betti map.
418	
419	
420	Next let us turn to the Betti form. Let $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ be the semi-positive $(1,1)$-form on $\mathfrak{A}_g$ as in Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiFormUnivAb}. Define $\omega:= \iota^*\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$. We will show that $\omega$ satisfies the properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}.
421	
422	The $(1,1)$-form $\omega$ is semi-positive as it is the pull-back of
423	the semi-positive form $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$. Moreover, it satisfies
424	$[N]^*\omega = N^2 \omega$ since $\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$ has this
425	property. Hence we have established properties (i) and (ii) of
426	Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}.
427	
428	Let us verify (iii) of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}. Suppose $X$ is
429	an irreducible subvariety of $\cA$ of dimension $d$.
430	Let $\Delta$ be an open subset of $\an{S}$ with
431	$\sman{X}\cap\cA_\Delta\not=\emptyset$; we may shrink $\Delta$ subject
432	to this condition. 
433	Let $Z
434	= \overline{\iota(X)}$ and observe $\dim Z \le d$. 
435	
436	Since $\omega = \iota^*\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}$, we have
437	\begin{equation}\label{EqOmegaAndOmegaUniv}
438	\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d} \not\equiv 0 \quad\text{if and only if}\quad \omega^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{Z}}^{\wedge d} \not\equiv 0.
439	\end{equation}
440	Next by definition of $b_{\Delta}$, we have the following property:
441	 For suitable non-empty open subsets $\Delta$ of $S^{\mathrm{an}}$ and
442	 $\Delta_0$ of $\mathbb{A}_g^{\mathrm{an}}$
443	 %$\overline{\iota_S(S)}^{\mathrm{an}}$
444	 such that $\iota_S(\Delta) \subseteq \Delta_0$, we have
445	\begin{equation}\label{EqBettiRankTrivialBound}
446	\max_{x \in \sman{X} \cap \cA_{\Delta}} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR}
447	(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{\sman{X}})_x
448	\le \max_{x \in \sman{Z} \cap \mathfrak{A}_{g,\Delta_0}} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta_0}|_{Z^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x \le 2\dim Z \le 2d.
449	\end{equation}
450	
451	Suppose first that $\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}\not\equiv 0$, then
452	\eqref{EqOmegaAndOmegaUniv} implies
453	$\omega^{\mathrm{univ}}|_{\sman{Z}}^{\wedge d}\not\equiv 0$ and in
454	particular $d=\dim Z$. 
455	We can apply 
456	Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}(iii)  to $Z$ and obtain
457	$ \max_{x \in \sman{Z} \cap \mathfrak{A}_{g,\Delta_0}} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR}
458	(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta_0}|_{Z^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x =2d$. 
459	Now $\iota|_X\colon X\rightarrow Z$ is
460	generically finite as $\dim X = \dim Z$,
461	so the first inequality in \eqref{EqBettiRankTrivialBound} is an
462	equality. We conclude
463	\begin{equation}
464	\label{eq:maxrankXgeneralA}
465	\max_{x \in \sman{X} \cap \cA_\Delta} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR}
466	(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{X^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x =2d.
467	\end{equation}
468	
469	
470	Conversely, assume \eqref{eq:maxrankXgeneralA} holds true.
471	Then we have equalities throughout in \eqref{EqBettiRankTrivialBound}.
472	By Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}(iii) applied to $Z$ and
473	by \eqref{EqOmegaAndOmegaUniv} we get $\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge
474	d}\not\equiv 0$.

1	%% Section 5: N\'{e}ron-Tate height and height on the base
2	
3	The goal of this section is to prove the full version of
4	Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}, \textit{i.e.}, without assuming {\tt
5	(Hyp)}. Let $S$ be an irreducible quasi-projective variety defined over $\IQbar$ and let $\pi\colon\cA\rightarrow S$ be an abelian scheme of relative dimension $g \ge 1$. 
6	
7	
8	Let $\cL$ be a relative ample line bundle on $\cA \rightarrow S$ with $[-1]^*\cL=\cL$, and
9	let $\cM$ be an ample line bundle on a compactification $\overline{S}$
10	of $S$. All data above are assumed to be defined over $\IQbar$. Set
11	$\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL} \colon \cA(\IQbar) \rightarrow \IR$ to be the
12	fiberwise N\'{e}ron--Tate height $\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL}(P) =
13	\hat{h}_{\cA_s,\cL_s}(P)$ with $s = \pi(P)$, and $h_{\overline{S},\cM}
14	\colon \overline{S}(\IQbar) \rightarrow \IR$ to be a representative of
15	 the height provided by the Height Machine; \textit{cf.} \cite[Chapter~2 and 9]{BG}.
16	
17	The main result of this appendix is the following theorem.
18	
19	\begin{theorem}\label{ThmHtInequalityFull}
20	Let $X$ be an irreducible subvariety of $\cA$
21	defined over $\IQbar$.   Suppose $X$ is non-degenerate,
22	as defined in Definition~\ref{DefinitionNonDegenerateApp}. Then there
23	exist constants $c_1>0$ and $c_2\ge 0$ and a Zariski open dense subset
24	$U$ of $X$ with %and a constant $c'$ such that
25	\begin{equation}\label{EqHtInequalityFull}
26	\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL}(P) \ge c_1 h_{\overline{S},\cM}( \pi(P) ) - c_2 \quad \text{for all}\quad P \in U(\IQbar).
27	\end{equation}
28	\end{theorem}
29	
30	Compared to Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}, $\cA \rightarrow S$ is no longer required to satisfy {\tt (Hyp)}. Other minor improvements are that $S$ is not required to be regular and $X$ is not required to be closed.
31	
32	In Definition~\ref{DefinitionNonDegenerate}, we defined non-degenerate subvarieties using the generic rank of the Betti map if $\cA \rightarrow S$ satisfies {\tt (Hyp)}. For an arbitrary $\cA \rightarrow S$, the definition is similar. But we need to first of all extend our construction of the Betti map, Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap}, to an arbitrary $\cA \rightarrow S$.
33	
34	
35	
36	
37	\subsection{Betti map}
38	In this subsection, we extend the construction of the Betti map, \textit{i.e.}, Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMap}, to an arbitrary $\cA \rightarrow S$ with $S$ regular. %, \textit{i.e.}, without the extra assumption {\tt (Hyp)}.
39	In this subsection, let $S$ be an  irreducible, regular, quasi-projective variety over $\IC$ and let $\pi \colon \cA \rightarrow S$ be an abelian scheme of relative dimension $g \ge 1$.
40	
41	
42	\begin{proposition}\label{PropBettiMapApp}
43	Let $s_0 \in S(\IC)$. Then there exist an open neighborhood $\Delta$ of $s_0$ in $S^{\mathrm{an}}$, and a map $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} := \pi^{-1}(\Delta) \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}$, called the Betti map, with the following properties.
44	\begin{enumerate}
45	\item[(i)] For each $s \in \Delta$ the restriction $b_{\Delta}|_{\cA_s(\IC)} \colon \cA_s(\IC) \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}$ is a group isomorphism.
46	\item[(ii)] For each $\xi \in \mathbb T^{2g}$ the preimage $b_{\Delta}^{-1}(\xi)$ is a complex analytic subset of $\cA_{\Delta}$.
47	\item[(iii)] The product
48	$(b_{\Delta},\pi) \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb
49	T^{2g} \times \Delta$ is a real analytic isomorphism.% Here $\widetilde{\pi} \colon \cA_{\widetilde{S}} \rightarrow \widetilde{S}$ is the natural projection.
50	\end{enumerate}
51	\end{proposition}

57	is uniquely determined  by properties (i) and (iii)
58	up-to the action of $\mathrm{GL}_{2g}(\IZ)$ if $\Delta$ is
59	connected.

61	% some subvariety.\Pcomment{I propose to strike the sentence ``In this
62	 %  paper\ldots'', it doesn't  seem to fit, and merge these two paragraphs.}
63	 Composing with an $\alpha \in \mathrm{GL}_{2g}(\IZ)$ does not change the rank. So by the discussion on the uniqueness above, any map $\cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ satisfying the three properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp} will be called \textit{Betti map}.%''  I
64	%  think this doesn't need a proof.

66	\begin{proof} Our proof of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp} follows the construction in \cite[$\mathsection$3-$\mathsection$4]{GaoBettiRank}. We divide it into several steps.
67	
68	By \cite[$\mathsection$2.1]{GenestierNgo}, $\cA \rightarrow S$ carries a polarization of type $D = \mathrm{diag}(d_1,\ldots,d_g)$ for some positive integers $d_1|d_2|\cdots|d_g$.
69	
70	%Let $D = \mathrm{diag}(d_1,\ldots,d_g)$ for some positive integers $d_1|d_2|\cdots|d_g$.
71	  \textbf{Case: Moduli space with level structure.} 
72	Fix $\ell \ge \bestlevel$ with $(\ell,d_g) = 1$. We start by proving Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp} for $S = \mathbb
73	A_{g,D,\ell}$, the moduli space of abelian varieties of
74	dimension $g$ polarized of type $D$ with level-$\ell$-structure. It is a fine moduli
75	space; see \cite[Theorem~2.3.1]{GenestierNgo}. Let $\pi^{\mathrm{univ}}_D \colon \mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell} \rightarrow \mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}$ be the universal abelian variety.
76	
77	The universal covering $\mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \mathbb
78	A_{g,D,\ell}^{\mathrm{an}}$ \cite[Proposition~1.3.2]{GenestierNgo},
79	where $\mathfrak{H}_g$ is the Siegel upper half space, gives a family of abelian varieties
80	$\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,D} \rightarrow \mathfrak{H}_g$ %, polarized of type $D$, 
81	fitting into the diagram
82	\[
83	\xymatrix{
84	\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,D} := \mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell} \times_{\mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}}\mathfrak{H}_g \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell}^{\mathrm{an}} \ar[d]^{\pi^{\mathrm{univ}}_D} \\
85	\mathfrak{H}_g \ar[r] & \mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}^{\mathrm{an}}.
86	}
87	\]
88	The family $\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,D} \rightarrow \mathfrak{H}_g$ is polarized of type $D$. 
89	For the universal covering
90	$u \colon \IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,D}$
91	and for each $Z\in \mathfrak{H}_g$, the kernel of
92	$u|_{\mathbb{C}^g \times \{Z\}}$ is $D\mathbb{Z}^g + Z \mathbb{Z}^g$.
93	Thus the map
94	$\IC^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \IR^g \times \IR^g \times \mathfrak{H}_g \rightarrow \IR^{2g}$,
95	where the first map is the inverse of $(a,b,Z) \mapsto (Da + Z  b,Z)$
96	and the second map is the natural projection, descends to a real
97	analytic map
98	\[
99	b^{\mathrm{univ}} \colon \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,D} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}.
100	\]
101	 Now for each $s_0 \in \mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}(\IC)$, there exists a
102	 contractible, relatively compact, open neighborhood $\Delta$ of $s_0$ in $\mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}^{\mathrm{an}}$ such that $\mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell,\Delta}:=(\pi^{\mathrm{univ}}_D)^{-1}(\Delta)$ can be identified with $\cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,\Delta'}$ for some open subset $\Delta'$ of $\mathfrak{H}_g$. The composite $b_{\Delta} \colon \mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell,\Delta} \cong \cA_{\mathfrak{H}_g,D,\Delta'} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}$ clearly  satisfies the three properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}. Thus $b_{\Delta}$ is the desired Betti map in this case.% Note that for a fixed (small enough) $\Delta$, there are infinitely choices of $\Delta'$; but for $\Delta$ small enough, if $\Delta_1'$ and $\Delta_2'$ are two such choices, then $\Delta_2' = \alpha \cdot \Delta_1'$ for some $\alpha \in \mathrm{Sp}_{2g}(\IZ) \subset \mathrm{SL}_{2g}(\IZ)$. Thus we have proven Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp} for $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow \mathbb A_g$.
103	
104	\medskip
105	  \textbf{Case: With level structure.} 
106	  Assume that $\cA \rightarrow S$ carries level-$\ell$-structure for some $\ell \ge \bestlevel$ with $(\ell,d_g) = 1$. 
107	As $\IA_{g,D,\ell}$ is a fine moduli space there exists a Cartesian diagram
108	\begin{equation*}%\label{EqEmbedAbSchIntoUnivAbVar}
109	\xymatrix{
110	\cA \ar[r]^-{\iota} \ar[d]_{\pi} \pullbackcorner & \mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell} \ar[d] \\
111	S \ar[r]^-{\iota_S} & \mathbb{A}_{g,D,\ell}.
112	}
113	\end{equation*}
114	
115	Now let $s_0 \in S(\IC)$. Applying Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}
116	to the universal abelian variety
117	$\mathfrak{A}_{g,D,\ell} \rightarrow \mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}$ and
118	$\iota_S(s_0) \in \mathbb A_{g,D,\ell}(\IC)$, we obtain an 
119	open neighborhood $\Delta_0$ of $\iota_S(s_0)$ in $\mathbb
120	A_{g,D,\ell}^{\mathrm{an}}$ and a real analytic map
121	\[
122	b_{\Delta_0} \colon \mathfrak{A}_{g,\Delta_0} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}
123	\]
124	satisfying the properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}.
125	
126	Now let $\Delta = \iota_S^{-1}(\Delta_0)$. Then $\Delta$ is an open neighborhood of $s_0$ in $S^{\mathrm{an}}$. Denote by $\cA_{\Delta} = \pi^{-1}(\Delta)$ and define
127	\[
128	b_{\Delta} = b_{\Delta_0} \circ \iota \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb T^{2g}.
129	\]
130	Then $b_{\Delta}$ satisfies the properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp} for $\cA \rightarrow S$. Hence $b_{\Delta}$ is our desired Betti map.
131

134	  \textbf{Case: General case.}
135	Let $s_0 \in S(\IC)$ and $\ell\ge\bestlevel$ be a prime with $(\ell,d_g)=1$.
136	
137	Fix any irreducible component $S_0$ of the kernel $\mathrm{ker}[\ell]$ of $[\ell] \colon
138	\cA\rightarrow \cA$. It is Zariski open in $\mathrm{ker}[\ell]$ as $S$ is
139	regular, so we consider it with its natural open subscheme structure.
140	Then $S_0\rightarrow \mathrm{ker}[\ell]$ is both a closed and open
141	immersion. So $S_0\rightarrow S$, the composition with
142	the finite \'etale morphism $\mathrm{ker}[\ell]\rightarrow S$, is finite and \'etale.
143	The upshot is that the base change of $\cA\rightarrow S$ by $S_0\rightarrow S$
144	admits an $\ell$-torsion section. After repeating this finitely many
145	times we obtain a finite and \'etale morphism $\rho \colon S'
146	\rightarrow S$ where $S'$ is irreducible and such that $\cA':=
147	\cA\times_S S' \rightarrow S'$ has level-$\ell$-structure.
148	Note that $S'$ is regular as $S$ is regular and regularity ascends
149	along \'etale morphisms. Moreover,
150	 $\cA' \rightarrow S'$ is still polarized of type $D$.

153	% S$, with $S'$ irreducible, such that $\cA':= \cA\times_S S' \rightarrow S'$ has
154	% level-$\ell$-structure.
155	% for some $\ell \ge \bestlevel$ with $(\ell,d_g)
156	% = 1$.

160	%  is normal, then $\mathrm{ker}[\ell]$ is normal as

166	%$S$ in Theorem B.1?}

168	Let $s_0' \in \rho^{-1}(s_0)$. Applying Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp} to $\cA' \rightarrow S'$ and $s_0' \in S'(\IC)$, we obtain an open neighborhood $\Delta'$ of $s_0'$ in $(S')^{\mathrm{an}}$ and a map $b_{\Delta'} \colon \cA'_{\Delta'} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ satisfying the properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}.
169	
170	Let $\Delta = \rho(\Delta')$. Up to shrinking $\Delta'$, we may assume that $\rho|_{\Delta'} \colon \Delta' \rightarrow \Delta$ is a homeomorphism and that $\Delta$ is an open neighborhood of $s_0$ in $S^{\mathrm{an}}$. Thus $\cA'_{\Delta'} \cong \cA_{\Delta}$. Now define
171	\[
172	b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}
173	\]
174	to be the composite of the inverse of $\cA'_{\Delta'} \cong \cA_{\Delta}$ and $b_{\Delta'}$. Then $b_{\Delta}$ is our desired Betti map.
175	\end{proof}

221	\subsection{Non-degenerate subvariety and
222	 Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}}
223	 We keep the notation as in the beginning of this appendix. 
224	\begin{definition}\label{DefinitionNonDegenerateApp}
225	An irreducible subvariety $X$ of $\cA$ is said to be
226	\textit{non-degenerate} if there exists an open non-empty subset
227	$\Delta$ of $S^{\mathrm{sm},\mathrm{an}}$,
228	   with the Betti map
229	$b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta}:=\pi^{-1}(\Delta) \rightarrow
230	\mathbb{T}^{2g}$ as in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}, such that
231	$X^\mathrm{sm,an}\cap \cA_{\Delta}\not=\emptyset$ and 
232	\[
233	\max_{x \in X^{\mathrm{sm}}(\IC) \cap \cA_{\Delta}} \mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{X^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x = 2\dim X.
234	\]%it satisfies one of the two equivalent conditions in property (iii) of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}.
235	\end{definition}

Let $\cL$ be a relative ample line bundle on $\cA \rightarrow S$, and let $\cM$ be an ample line bundle on a compactification $\overline{S}$ of $S$. All data above are assumed to be defined over $\IQbar$. Define $\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL} \colon \cA(\IQbar) \rightarrow \IR$ to be $\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL}(P) = \hat{h}_{\cA_s,\cL_s}(P)$ with $s = \pi(P)$, and $h_{S,\cM} \colon S(\IQbar) \rightarrow \IR$ to a height function.

250	\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull}]
251	  Let $\ell\ge 3$ be a prime.
252	  We will  reduce the current theorem to Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}
253	  by successively assuming, in addition to the hypothesis of Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull}, that
254	\begin{enumerate}
255	  \item[(i)] $X$ is Zariski closed in $\cA$,
256	  \item[(ii)] $\pi|_X \colon X\rightarrow S$ is dominant,
257	  \item[(iii)] $S$ is regular, 
258	  \item[(iv)] $\cA \rightarrow S$ is $S$-isogenous to an abelian scheme which carries a principal polarization,
259	  \item[(v)] $\cA \rightarrow S$ carries a principal polarization,
260	  \item[(vi)] $\cA$ carries a level $\ell$-structure, and
261	  \item[(vii)]  we have the same hypothesis as Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}.% $\cA\rightarrow S$ furthermore is equipped with a closed
262	    % immersion $\cA \rightarrow \IP^n_{\IQbar} \times S$ over $S$
263	    % as in the beginning of $\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}
264	    % and
265	    % a closed immersion
266	    % $\overline S\rightarrow \IP^m_{\IQbar}$.
267	%
268	    % \item[(iv)] $X$ is closed.
269	  % \item[(i)] $S$ is regular, 
270	  % \item[(ii)] $\cA \rightarrow S$ satisfies {\tt (Hyp)} (carries a principal polarization and has
271	  %   level-$\ell$-structure for some $\ell \ge \bestlevel$),
272	  % \item[(iii)] $\cA\rightarrow S$ furthermore is equipped with a closed immersion $\cA \rightarrow \IP^n_{\IQbar} \times S$ as in the beginning of $\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq},
273	  % \item[(iv)] $X$ is closed.
274	\end{enumerate}
275	We will proceed the proof with six d\'{e}vissage steps.
276	In d\'evissage step $n$ we will deduce
277	the theorem under the hypotheses (i),\ldots,($n-1$) 
278	from the theorem under the hypotheses (i),\ldots,($n$). 
279	
280	% In each d\'{e}vissage step, all assumptions from the previous d\'{e}vissage steps are assumed to be satisfied.
281	
282	\ul{First d\'{e}vissage: reduction to the case where $X$ is Zariski
283	  closed in $\cA$.}
284	
285	Let $\overline{X}$ denote the
286	Zariski closure of $X$ in $\cA$. Then $X$ 
287	is a Zariski open dense subset of $\overline{X}$ and $\dim X = \dim
288	\overline{X}$. % Therefore $X$ is non-degenerate if and only if
289	% $\overline{X}$ is non-degenerate by
290	% Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiRankZarOpenApp}.
291	Therefore, $\overline X$ is non-degenerate if $X$ is non-degenerate.
292	Now if
293	\eqref{EqHtInequalityFull} holds true on a Zariski open dense subset
294	$U$ of $\overline{X}$, then \eqref{EqHtInequalityFull} clearly holds
295	true on $U \cap X$, which is Zariski open and dense in $X$. Thus it suffices to prove \eqref{EqHtInequalityFull} with $X$ replaced by $\overline{X}$. %So we may assume that (iv) is furthermore satisfied.
296	
297	
298	\ul{Second d\'{e}vissage: reduction to the case where $\pi|_{X}\colon X\rightarrow 
299	  S$ is dominant.} 
300	  
301	  As $X$ is non-degenerate, there exists a non-empty open subset $\Delta$ of $S^{\mathrm{sm,an}}$, with Betti map $b_{\Delta}$, such that $\mathrm{rank}_{\IR}(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{X^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x = 2 \dim X$ for some $x \in X^{\mathrm{sm}}(\IC) \cap \cA_{\Delta}$.
302	
303	Endow the Zariski closed set $S'=\pi(X)$ with the reduced induced
304	subscheme structure and set $\cA' = \cA \times_S S' =
305	\pi^{-1}(S')$. Then $X \times_S S'$ identifies with $X$ via the natural projection $\cA' \rightarrow \cA$. Hence there exists a non-empty open subset $\Delta'$
306	of $(S')^{\mathrm{sm,an}}$ with $\pi(x) \in \Delta'\subset \Delta$ and
307	\[
308	 \mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta'}|_{X^{\mathrm{sm,an}}})_x = 2\dim X.
309	\]
310	Thus $X$ is a non-degenerate subvariety of $\cA'$. On the other hand,
311	the conclusion of  Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull} does not change
312	with $\cA \rightarrow S$ replaced by $\cA' \rightarrow S'$, $\cL$
313	replaced by $\cL|_{\cA'}$ and $\cM$ replaced by $\cM|_{\overline{S'}}$,
314	 where $\overline{S'}$ is the Zariski closure of $S'$ in $\overline{S}$.
315	Hence it suffices to prove Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull} after
316	these replacements and thus we may assume that $X$ dominates $S$.
317	
318	
319	\ul{Third d\'{e}vissage: reduction to the case where $S$ is regular.}
320	
321	Recall that $S^{\mathrm{sm}}$ is the regular locus of $S$.
322	Now $\pi|_X\colon X\rightarrow S$ is dominant, so
323	$X'=X\cap \pi^{-1}(S^{\mathrm{sm}})$ is Zariski open and dense in
324	$X$. 
325	Since $X$ is non-degenerate it follows by definition that  $X'$ is
326	non-degenerate. Moreover, the conclusion of
327	Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull} does not change if we replace $\cA
328	\rightarrow S$ by $\cA' = \pi^{-1}(S^{\mathrm{sm}})
329	\rightarrow S^{\mathrm{sm}}$, $\cL$ by $\cL|_{\cA'}$,
330	and $X$ by $X'$.
331	Finally, observe that $X'$ is Zariski closed in $\cA'$
332	and $\pi(X') = \pi(X)\cap S^{\mathrm{reg}}$, so $\pi|_{X'}\colon X'
333	\rightarrow S^{\mathrm{reg}}$ is dominant. 
334	%So we may assume that (i) is satisfied.
335	
336	
337	\ul{Fourth d\'{e}vissage: reduction to the case where $\pi \colon \cA \rightarrow S$ is $S$-isogenous to an abelian scheme which carries a principal polarization.}
338	
339	%satisfies {\tt (Hyp')}, with}
340	%\begin{center}
341	%{\tt (Hyp'):} 
342	%it has level-$\ell$-structure for some $\ell \ge \bestlevel$, and for its generic fiber $A$ there exists an isogenous $A_0 \rightarrow A$ defined over $\IQbar(S)$ with $A_0$ principally polarized.
343	%\end{center}
344	
345	%There exists a quasi-finite \'{e}tale dominant %\Pcomment{Replace finite by quasi-finite?}
346	%morphism $S' \rightarrow S$ such that
347	%$\cA':= \cA\times_S S' \rightarrow S'$ has level-$\ell$-structure for
348	%some $\ell \ge \bestlevel$. 
349	By \cite[$\mathsection$23,
350	Corollary~1]{MumfordAbVar}, each abelian variety over an algebraic
351	closed field is isogenous to a principally polarized one. Applying
352	this to the geometric generic fiber of $\cA \rightarrow S$, we obtain a
353	quasi-finite \'{e}tale dominant morphism 
354	 $\rho \colon S' \rightarrow S$ with $S'$ irreducible and the
355	 following property: There exists a principally polarized $A'_0$ that
356	 is isogenous over $\IQbar(S')$ to  the
357	 generic fiber $A'$ of $\cA':= \cA\times_{S} S' \rightarrow S'$. 
358	 Up to replacing $S'$ by an open dense subscheme, we may furthermore assume that $A'_0$ extends to an abelian scheme $\cA'_0 \rightarrow S'$. 
359	 Denote by $\rho_{\cA} \colon \cA' =\cA \times_S S' \rightarrow \cA$ the natural projection; it is a quasi-finite \'{e}tale dominant morphism.
360	
361	As regularity ascends along \'etale morphisms and as $S$ is regular we conclude that $S'$ is regular. Thus $\cA'_0 \rightarrow S'$ carries a principal polarization by \cite[Th\'{e}or\`{e}me~XI~1.4]{LNM119}, and the isogeny $A'_0 \rightarrow A'$ extends to an $S'$-isogeny $\cA'_0 \rightarrow \cA'$ by \cite[Lemme~XI~1.15]{LNM119}.% Thus  the isogeny from $A'_0$ to the generic fiber of $\cA' \rightarrow S'$ extends to an $S'$-isogeny $\cA'_0 \rightarrow \cA'$, \textit{cf.} \cite[Th\'{e}or\`{e}me~XI~1.4]{LNM119}.% The morphism $\lambda$ is a finite morphism, and hence $\lambda \circ \rho_{\cA} \colon \cA'_0 \rightarrow \cA$ is quasi-finite dominant.
362	
363	
364	There is  an irreducible component $X'$ of $\rho_{\cA}^{-1}(X)$ with
365	$\dim X' = \dim X$. Then $X'$ is Zariski closed in $\cA'$, 
366	the image $\rho_{\cA}(X')$ is Zariski dense in $X$,  and thus $X'$ dominates $S'$ (it even surjects to $S'$
367	since $\cA'\rightarrow S'$ is proper and $X'$ is closed). We
368	claim that $X'$, as a subvariety of $\cA'$, is non-degenerate.
369	Indeed, $\rho_{\cA}(X')$ contains a Zariski open dense subset $U$ of
370	$X$. Since $X$ is a non-degenerate
371	subvariety of $\cA$, so is $U$ by
372	Lemma~\ref{LemmaBettiRankZarOpenApp}. So there exists an open subset
373	$\Delta$ of $\an{S}$ with the Betti map $b_{\Delta} \colon
374	\cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ such that
375	$\mathrm{rank}_{\IR}(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{\ansm{U}})_u = 2 \dim U =
376	2 \dim X$ %\Pinlinecomment{
377	for all $u$ from a non-empty open subset of $\an{U}$
378	%}
379	. Take $\Delta'$ to be a connected component of $\rho^{-1}(\Delta)$ such that $X'
380	\cap (\pi')^{-1}(\Delta') \not= \emptyset$. Set $\cA'_{\Delta'} =
381	(\pi')^{-1}(\Delta')$, and replace $\Delta$ by $\rho(\Delta')$.
382	Note that $\rho|_{\Delta'}\colon \Delta' \cong \Delta$ is then
383	bianalytic after possibly shrinking $\Delta'$ (and so is $\rho_{\cA}\colon \cA'_{\Delta'} \cong
384	\cA_{\Delta}$). Now $b_{\Delta} \circ \rho_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}} \colon \cA'_{\Delta'} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ satisfies the three properties listed in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}. So $b_{\Delta} \circ \rho_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}}$ is the Betti map, which we denote for simplicity by $b_{\Delta'}$; see below Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}. 
385	For $u' \in  (\rho_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}})^{-1}(u)\cap \an{X'}$
386	 and for sufficiently general $u$%\Pcomment{Slight
387	%  elaboration as $\rho|_{X'}$ must have maximal rank at $u$}
388	, we have 
389	$\mathrm{rank}_{\IR}(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta'}|_{\ansm{X'}})_{u'} = 2 \dim X$. So $X'$, as a subvariety of the abelian scheme $\cA'$ over $S'$, is non-degenerate.
390	
391	Now we have a non-degenerate subvariety $X'$ of the abelian scheme $\pi' \colon \cA' \rightarrow S'$. The line bundle $\rho_{\cA}^*\cL$ on $\cA'$ is relatively ample. Suppose that $\cM'$ is an ample line bundle on some compactification $\overline{S'}$ of $S'$.
392	
393	Assume that Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull} holds for $\pi' \colon \cA' \rightarrow S'$, $\rho_{\cA}^*\cL$, $\cM'$, and $X'$. Thus there exist constants $c_1'>0$, $c_2' \ge 0$ and a Zariski open non-empty subset $U'$ of $X'$ with
394	\[
395	\hat{h}_{\cA',\rho_{\cA}^*\cL}(P') \ge c_1' h_{\overline{S'},\cM'}(\pi'(P')) - c_2' \quad \text{for all} \quad P' \in U'(\IQbar).
396	\]
397	Denote by $P = \rho_{\cA}(P')$. 
398	By the Height Machine we have $\hat{h}_{\cA',\rho_{\cA}^*\cL}(P') =
399	\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL}(P)$.%\Pinlinecomment{We have to be more careful when
400	%  applying the height machine. Our reference
401	%  Bombieri-Gubler doesn't define the  HM when the variety in
402	%question is only quasi-projective. Projectiveness ist crucial for the
403	%functoriality properties.} \Gcomment{The $\hat{h}$ is fiberwise
404	%defined in our convention. As fiberwise the variety is projective, I
405	%think it's ok to apply the Height Machine
406	%directly.}\Pinlinecomment{This argument may work for $\hat h$, but not
407	%for $h_{S'',\cM''}$ for which  we freely apply HM arguments below. I
408	%think it's easy to fix this, will look into it later.} \Ginlinecomment{I don't think we apply HM to $h_{S'',\cM''}$ below. I changed the notation on height function $h_{S,\cM}$ to $h_{\overline{S},\cM}$ to emphasize that $\cM$ is an ample line bundle on $\overline{S}$ and not just on $S$.}
409	
410	By  \cite[Lemma~4]{Silverman:heightest11} applied to $\rho \colon S' \rightarrow S$ and the line bundles $\cM'$ and $\cM$, there exist $c' = c' (\rho,\cM',\cM) > 0$ and $c'' = c''(\rho,\cM',\cM) \ge 0$ such that $h_{\overline{S'},\cM'}(\pi'(P')) \ge c' h_{\overline{S},\cM}(\rho(\pi'(P'))) - c'' = c' h_{\overline{S},\cM}(\pi(P)) - c''$ for all $P' \in \cA'(\IQbar)$. Hence the height inequality above implies
411	\[
412	\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL}(P) \ge c_1'c' h_{\overline{S},\cM}(\pi(P)) - (c_1'c'' + c_2')\quad \text{for all}\quad P \in \rho_{\cA}(U')(\IQbar).
413	\]
414	Now that $\rho_{\cA}(U')$ contains a Zariski open non-empty (hence dense) subset $U$ of $X$ by Chevalley's Theorem. 
415	Thus Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull} also holds true for $\pi \colon \cA \rightarrow S$, $\cL$, $\cM$, and $X$.
416	
417	In summary, we have shown that it suffices to prove Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequalityFull} for $\pi' \colon \cA' \rightarrow S'$, $\rho_{\cA}^*\cL$, $\cM'$ and $X'$. Thus we are reduced to the case where the generic fiber of $\cA \rightarrow S$ is isogenous to a principally polarized abelian variety.%carries a principal polarization.

420	\ul{Fifth d\'{e}vissage: reduction to the case where $\pi \colon \cA \rightarrow S$ carries a principal polarization.}
421	
422	From the previous d\'{e}vissage, there exists a principally polarized abelian scheme $\pi_0 \colon \cA_0 \rightarrow S$ with an $S$-isogeny $\lambda \colon \cA_0 \rightarrow \cA$. Note that $\lambda$ is a finite \'{e}tale morphism. The line bundle $\lambda^*\cL$ on $\cA_0$ is relatively ample. By the Height Machine we have $\hat{h}_{\cA_0,\lambda^*\cL}(P') = \hat{h}_{\cA,\cL}(\lambda(P'))$ for all $P' \in \cA_0(\IQbar)$.
423	
424	There is  an irreducible component $X_0$ of $\lambda^{-1}(X)$ with
425	$\dim X_0 = \dim X$. Then $X_0$ is Zariski closed in $\cA_0$ and thus $X_0$ dominates $S$ (it even surjects to $S$
426	since $X = \lambda(X_0)$). We
427	claim that $X_0$, as a subvariety of $\cA_0$, is non-degenerate. Assume this. Then it suffices to prove the height inequality \eqref{EqHtInequalityFull} with $\cA \rightarrow S$ replaced by $\cA_0 \rightarrow S$, $X$ replaced by $X_0$, and $\cL$ replaced by $\lambda^*\cL$. 
428	
429	It remains to prove that $X_0$ is a non-degenerate subvariety of $\cA_0$. To do this, we need some preparation on Betti maps. Let $\Delta$ be an open subset of $S^{\mathrm{an}}$ with the Betti map $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$. Set $\cA_{0,\Delta} = \pi_0^{-1}(\Delta)$, and denote by $\lambda_{\Delta}$ the restriction of $\lambda \colon \cA_0 \rightarrow \cA$ to $\cA_{0,\Delta}$. Up to shrinking $\Delta$ we have a Betti map $b_{0,\Delta} \colon \cA_{0,\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$. By property (iii) of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}, we have two real analytic isomorphisms $(b_{0,\Delta},\pi_0) \colon \cA_{0,\Delta} \cong \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \Delta$ and $(b_{\Delta},\pi) \colon \cA_{\Delta} \cong \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \Delta$. Thus there exists a real analytic map $\lambda' \colon \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \Delta \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \Delta$ such that the following diagram commutes
430	\[
431	\xymatrix{
432	\cA_{0,\Delta} \ar[r]^-{(b_{0,\Delta},\pi_0)}_-{\sim} \ar[d]_-{\lambda_{\Delta}} & \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \Delta \ar[d]^-{\lambda'} \\
433	\cA_{\Delta} \ar[r]^-{(b_{\Delta},\pi)}_-{\sim} & \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \Delta.
434	}
435	\]
436	As $\lambda$ is a finite map, $(\lambda')^{-1}(r)$ is a finite set for each $r \in \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times\Delta$. As $\lambda$ is an $S$-morphism, for each $s \in \Delta$ we have $\lambda'({\mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \{s\}}) \subseteq \mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \{s\}$.
437	
438	By property (i) of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiMapApp}, for each $s \in \Delta$ the restriction $\lambda'|_{\mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \{s\}}$ is a group homomorphism $\mathbb{T}^{2g}  \rightarrow  \mathbb{T}^{2g}$. Thus $\ker(\lambda'|_{\mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \{s\}})$ is a finite, hence discrete, subgroup of $\mathbb{T}^{2g}$. In particular, $\ker(\lambda'|_{\mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \{s\}})$ is locally constant. Up to shrinking $\Delta$, we may assume $\ker(\lambda'|_{\mathbb{T}^{2g} \times \{s\}}) = H$ for each $s \in \Delta$. Set $\lambda_{\mathbb{T}} \colon \mathbb{T}^{2g} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ the quotient by the finite subgroup $H$. 
439	Then the diagram above induces a commutative diagram
440	\begin{equation}
441	\xymatrix{
442	\cA_{0,\Delta} \ar[r]^-{b_{0,\Delta}} \ar[d]_-{\lambda_{\Delta}} & \mathbb{T}^{2g} \ar[d]^-{\lambda_{\mathbb{T}}} \\
443	\cA_{\Delta} \ar[r]^-{b_{\Delta}} & \mathbb{T}^{2g}.
444	}
445	\end{equation}
446	Note that $\lambda_{\mathbb{T}}$ is a local homeomorphism.
447	
448	
449	Now we turn to proving that $X_0$ is non-degenerate in $\cA_0$. Indeed, as $X$ is a non-degenerate subvariety of $\cA$, there exists an open subset $\Delta$ of $S^{\mathrm{an}}$ with the Betti map $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ such that $\mathrm{rank}_{\IR}(\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{X^{\mathrm{an,sm}}})_x = 2 \dim X$ for all $x$ from a non-empty open subset of $X^{\mathrm{an}}$. For $x_0 \in  \lambda^{-1}(x)\cap X_0^{\mathrm{an}}$
450	 and for sufficiently general $x$%\Pcomment{Slight
451	%  elaboration as $\rho|_{X'}$ must have maximal rank at $u$}
452	, we have 
453	$\mathrm{rank}_{\IR}(\mathrm{d}(\lambda_{\mathbb{T}}\circ b_{0,\Delta})|_{X_0^{\mathrm{an,sm}}})_{x_0} = 2 \dim X = 2 \dim X_0$. But $\lambda_{\mathbb{T}}$ is a local homeomorphism, so $\mathrm{rank}_{\IR}(\mathrm{d} b_{0,\Delta}|_{X_0^{\mathrm{an,sm}}})_{x_0} = 2 \dim X_0$. Thus $X_0$ is non-degenerate.
454	
455	
456

489	X$, and $\cL$ replaced by $\rho_{\cA}^*\cL$, and $\cM$ replaced by

492	  % h_{\cA',\rho_{\cA}^*\cL} = \hat
493	  % h_{\cA,\cL}\circ \rho_{\cA}$ by the Height Machine).

500	\ul{Seventh d\'{e}vissage: reduction to  Theorem {\ref{ThmHtInequality}}.}
501	
502	It remains to prove the height inequality (\ref{EqHtInequalityFull})
503	with the extra hypotheses (i) - (v) listed above using
504	Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}. In this theorem we assumed in addition that 
505	the
506	fiberwise  N\'eron--Tate height on $\cA(\IQbar)$
507	is induced by  a closed immersion $\cA \rightarrow \IP^n_{\IQbar}
508	\times S$ satisfying the second and third bullet  at the beginning of
509	$\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq} and
510	that the height on $\overline S(\IQbar)$ is the restriction of the absolute
511	logarthmic Weil height coming from a closed immersion
512	$\overline S\rightarrow\IP^m_{\IQbar}$. 
513	% % \Pinlinecomment{There may be some
514	% %  overlap with Remark \ref{rmk:projembedding}}
515	% First of all note that the fourth bullet point already holds true.
516	% Indeed, \texttt{(Hyp)} were handled in the fourth and fifth d\'evissages.
517	
518	%\Pcomment{I slightly reworded the next paragraph.}
519	A basis of the global sections of the line bundle $\cM^{\otimes p}$,
520	for some $p$ large enough, gives rise to a closed immersion $\overline S \subseteq
521	\IP_{\IQbar}^m$. This gives the first bullet point at the beginning of
522	$\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}. Note that the Weil
523	height $h$ on $\IP_\IQbar^m(\IQbar)$ restricted to $\overline
524	S(\IQbar)$ via this immersion differs from 
525	$p h_{\overline S,\cM}$ by a bounded function.
526	% on $S$ given by this immersion $S \subseteq \IP^m$, we have
527	% $h_{\overline S,\cM} = (1/p)h$ up to a bounded function on $S(\IQbar)$.
528	
529	For the line bundle $\cL$ on $\cA$, which is ample relative over $S$, we have that $\cL^{\otimes 4}$ is relatively very ample on $\cA/S$. Thus by  \cite[Proposition~4.4.10.(ii) and Proposition~4.1.4]{EGAII}, there is a closed immersion $\cA \rightarrow \IP^n_S = \IP^n \times S$ given by global sections of $\cL^{\otimes 4} \otimes \pi^*\cM^{\otimes q}$ for some large $q$. When restricted to the generic fiber $A$ of $\cA \rightarrow S$, we get a closed immersion $A \rightarrow \IP^n_{k(S)}$ which arises from a basis of the global sections of $L^{\otimes 4}$, where $L$ is the restriction of $\cL$ over the generic fiber $A$. Moreover $L$ is ample since $\cL$ is relatively ample, and $L$ is symmetric since $[-1]^*\cL = \cL$. Thus we also have the second and third bullet points at the beginning of $\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}.
530	%Let $\eta$ be the generic fiber of $\cA \rightarrow S$. The desired immersion can be obtained by applying Remark~\ref{rmk:projembedding} to $L_0 = \cL_{\eta}$; we point out that the $\cL$, $\cM$ in Remark~\ref{rmk:projembedding} are different from our $\cL$, $\cM$ here.

533	\[
534	\hat{h}_{\cA} \colon \cA(\IQbar) \rightarrow [0,\infty), \quad P \rightarrow \hat{h}_{\cA_s,\cL_s^{\otimes 4}}(P)
535	\]
536	where $s = \pi(P)$. So $\hat{h}_{\cA,\cL} = (1/4)\hat{h}_{\cA}$. 
537	
538	
539	The full
540	hypothesis of Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}  is now satisfied for $\cA$
541	and $X$,
542	\textit{e.g.}, \texttt{(Hyp)} is just (iv) and (v).
543	We get constants $c_1> 0$ and $c_2$ and a Zariski open dense subset $U$ of $X$ such that
544	\[
545	    \hat{h}_{\cA}(P) \ge c_1 h( \pi(P) ) - c_2 \quad \text{for all}\quad P \in U(\IQbar).
546	\]
547	Thus \eqref{EqHtInequalityFull} holds true with $c_1$ replaced by $(c_1p)/4$ and $c_2$ replaced by $c_2/4 + O_S(1)$, where $O_S(1)$ is a bounded function on $S(\IQbar)$. So we are done.
548	\end{proof}

1	% Appendix A:
2	%\subsection{Introduction}
3	%\subsection{Model of an Abelian Scheme}
4	
5	Our goal in this appendix is to present a treatment of the
6	Silverman--Tate Theorem, \cite[Theorem~A]{Silverman}, using the language
7	of Cartier divisors. Using Cartier divisors as opposed to Weil
8	divisors  allows us to relax the flatness 
9	hypotheses imposed on $\pi$ in the notation of \cite[$\mathsection$3]{Silverman}. Apart from this minor tweak
10	we closely follow the original argument presented by Silverman. 
11	
12	Suppose $S$ is a {regular},
13	{irreducible}, {quasi-projective} variety over $\IQbar$.
14	Let $\pi \colon \cA\rightarrow S$ be an abelian scheme.
15	We write $\eta$ for the generic point of $S$ and $\cA_\eta$ for the
16	generic fiber of $\pi$. Then $\cA_\eta$ is an abelian variety defined
17	over $\IQbar(\eta)$. 
18	
19	Suppose we are presented with a closed immersion
20	$\cA\rightarrow \IP_{\IQbar}^n\times S$ over $S$ and
21	 with a projective variety $\overline S$
22	containing $S$ as a Zariski open and dense subset.
23	We will assume that $\overline S$ is embedded into $\IP_\IQbar^m$.
24	We do not assume that $\overline S$ is regular. % Let $\cM$ be a fixed
25	% ample line bundle on $\overline S$.
26	
27	We identify $\cA$ with a subvariety of $\IP_\IQbar^n\times S$.
28	Moreover, let $\overline{\cA}$ denote the Zariski closure of 
29	$\cA$ in $\IP_S^n=\IP_{\IQbar}^n\times\overline S \subset
30	\IP_{\IQbar}^n\times\IP_\IQbar^m$. %% We consider $S$
31	%% as Zariski open in $\overline S$ and $\cA$ as Zariski open in
32	%% $\overline \cA$.
33	
34	We set $\overline\cL = \cO(1,1)|_{\overline{\cA}}$ and  $\cL
35	= \overline\cL|_{\cA}$. We  will  assume in addition that 
36	$[-1]^*\cL_\eta \cong \cL_\eta$ 
37	where  $\cL_\eta$ is the restriction of $\cL$ to $\cA_\eta$. 
38	This implies
39	$[2]^*\cL_\eta \cong \cL_\eta^{\otimes 4}$.
40	
41	\newcommand{\hcA}{h}
42	
43	Given these immersions, we have several height functions.
44	For
45	$(P,s)\in \overline{\cA}(\IQbar)\subset \IP^n_\IQbar(\IQbar)\times \IP^m_\IQbar(\IQbar)$ we define
46	$\hcA(P,s) = h(P)+h(s)$ using the Weil height.
47	Moreover, for $s\in \overline S(\IQbar)\subset \IP^m_\IQbar(\IQbar)$
48	we define $h_{\overline S}(s) = h(s)$. 
49	Finally, 
50	for all  $P\in \cA(\IQbar)$ we denote by
51	  \begin{equation*}
52	    \hat h_{\cA}(P) = \lim_{N\rightarrow \infty}
53	    \frac{\hcA([N](P))}{N^2}
54	  \end{equation*}
55	the N\'eron--Tate height with respect to $\cL$;  it is
56	well-known that the limit converges, \textit{cf.} the reference around
57	(\ref{eq:fiberwiseNT}). 
58	  
59	We will prove the following variant of the Silverman--Tate Theorem.
60	
61	\begin{theorem}
62	  \label{thm:silvermantate}
63	  There exists a constant $c>0$  such that for all 
64	  $P \in \cA(\IQbar)$ we have 
65	  \begin{equation*}
66	    \bigl| \hat h_{\cA}(P) -
67	    \hcA(P)\bigr| \le c  \max\{1,h_{\overline
68	      S}(\pi(P))\}. 
69	  \end{equation*}
70	\end{theorem}
71	
72	The constant $c$ depends on $\cA$ and on the various immersions but not on $P$. 
73	The proof is distributed over  the  next subsections.
74

176	    \label{eq:duplicationbound}

178	   \hcA(P)\right|  \le c_1  \max\{1,h_{\overline
179	   S}(\pi(P))\}
180	  \end{equation}
181	  holds for all $P\in \cA(\IQbar)$. 
182	\end{proposition}
183	\begin{proof}
184	  We define
185	  \begin{equation}
186	  \label{def:cF2}
187	  \cF' =  [2]^* \overline{\cL} \otimes
188	  \rho^*\overline{\cL}^{\otimes(-4)} \in \mathrm{Pic}(\overline{\cA}'). 
189	\end{equation}
190	  
191	Recall that we have identified $\cA$ with a Zariski open subset of
192	$\overline{\cA}'$. 
193	The  restriction of $[2]^*\overline{\cL}$ to the generic fiber
194	$\cA_\eta\subset\cA\subset\overline{\cA}'$
195	coincides with $[2]^*\cL_\eta$ and the restriction of $\rho^*\overline{\cL}$  to $\cA_\eta$
196	is identified with $\cL_\eta$. Using our assumption
197	 $[2]^*\cL_\eta \cong
198	\cL_\eta^{\otimes 4}$ on the generic fiber $\cA_\eta$ we see that  $\cF'$ is trivial on $\cA_\eta$.

200	By \cite[Corollaire~21.4.13 (pp.~361 of EGA IV-4, in Errata et Addenda, liste~3)]{EGAIV} %% (Errata et appenda)
201	applied to
202	$\cA\rightarrow S$ there exists a line
203	bundle $\cM$ on $S$ such that $\pi|_{\cA}^*\cM \cong \cF'|_{\cA}$.

206	Hironaka's Theorem. Thus there is a proper, birational morphism
207	$b\colon \overline S'\rightarrow\overline S$ that is an isomorphism
208	above $S$
209	 such that  $\overline S'$
210	is regular. We consider $S$ as Zariski open in $\overline S'$.  Note that $b$ is even
211	projective and $\overline S'$ is integral. So $\overline S'$ is an
212	irreducible, regular, projective variety. 
213	
214	Now consider the base change $\overline
215	\cA'\times_{\overline S}\overline S'$. This new scheme
216	may  fail to be irreducible or even
217	reduced. However, recall that $b$ is an isomorphism above the regular $S\subset
218	\overline S$. So $(\overline
219	\cA'\times_{\overline S}\overline S')_S =
220	%\overline{\cA}'\times_{\overline{S}'}S=
221	\overline{\cA}'\times_{\overline S}S$  is isomorphic to $\cA$ and thus
222	integral. We may consider $\cA$ as an open subscheme of
223	$\overline
224	\cA'\times_{\overline S}\overline S'$. It must be contained in an irreducible component of
225	$\overline
226	\cA'\times_{\overline S}\overline S'$. We endow this irreducible component with the
227	reduced induced structure and obtain an integral, closed subscheme
228	$\overline{\overline{\cA}}\subset \overline
229	\cA'\times_{\overline S}\overline S'$. We get a commutative diagram
230	\begin{equation*}
231	  \xymatrix{
232	\cA\ar@{}[r]|-*[@]{\subset}\ar[d]_\pi &
233	 \overline{\overline{\cA}} \ar[r]^f \ar[d]_{\overline\pi} & \overline{\cA}' \ar[d]_{\pi\circ\rho}&\\
234	S \ar@{}[r]|-*[@]{\subset} &\overline{S}' \ar[r] &      \overline{S} &
235	  }  
236	\end{equation*}
237	The horizontal  morphisms compose to the identity on the domain. % We write $f\colon \overline{\overline\cA}\rightarrow \overline \cA'$ for
238	% the natural morphism. 
239	% Its image in
240	% $\overline{S}'$ contains the open subset $\beta_1^{-1}(S)$. As $\overline S'$
241	% is irreducible, we find by properness that
242	% $\overline\pi\colon\overline{\overline{\cA}}\rightarrow\overline S'$ is surjective, it is
243	% also projective. 
244	
245	We consider $S$ as a Zariski open subset of $\overline S'$. 
246	As $\overline S'$ is regular, we can extend $\cM$ to a line bundle on
247	the regular $\overline S'$, \textit{cf.} \cite[Corollary~11.41]{GoertzWedhorn}. % G\"ortz--Wedhorn.
248	The pull-back $f^*\cF'\otimes\overline\pi^*\cM^{\otimes(-1)}$ is
249	trivial on $\cA\subset\overline{\overline{\cA}}$. 
250	
251	By Hironaka's Theorem there is a proper, birational morphism $\beta\colon\widetilde
252	{\cA}\rightarrow\overline{\overline\cA}$ that is an isomorphism above $\cA$
253	(which is regular) 
254	such that $\widetilde{\cA}$ is regular. We may identify $\cA$ with a Zariski open subset of
255	$\widetilde \cA$.
256	%But it may not be trivial on $\widetilde \cA$. 
257	
258	Now we pull everything back to the regular  $\widetilde\cA$.
259	More precisely, we set $\cF = \beta^*f^*\cF'$.
260	% We consider $\cA$ as a Zariski open subset of $\widetilde\cA$.
261	Then
262	$\cF\otimes\beta^*\overline\pi^*\cM^{\otimes(-1)}$ is trivial when restricted to
263	$\cA$. 
264	
265	To a Cartier divisor $D$ we attach its line bundle $\cO(D)$.
266	As $\widetilde \cA$ is integral we may fix a Cartier divisor $D$ on
267	$\widetilde \cA$ with $\cO(D) \cong \cF\otimes \beta^*\overline\pi^*
268	\cM^{\otimes(-1)}$. Let $\mathrm{cyc}(D)$ denote the Weil divisor of $\widetilde\cA$
269	attached to $D$. The linear equivalence class of $\mathrm{cyc}(D)$
270	restricted to $\cA$ is trivial. By \cite[Proposition~11.40]{GoertzWedhorn}
271	%% \footnote{This proposition is false as stated, one
272	%%   needs \textit{e.g.} in addition that $X=\widetilde{\cA}$ is reduced. Recall
273	%%   that the  blowup of a reduced scheme is reduced.}
274	$\mathrm{cyc}(D)$ is linearly equivalent to a Weil divisor $\sum_{i=1}^r n_i
275	Z_i$ with  $Z_i \subset\widetilde{\cA}\ssm \cA$ irreducible and of
276	codimension $1$ in $\widetilde{\cA}$. 
277	
278	We let $\widetilde\pi$ denote the composition
279	$\widetilde\cA\rightarrow \overline{\overline\cA}\rightarrow \overline{S}'$. 
280	Let us consider $\widetilde\pi(Z_i) = Y_i$. As $\widetilde\pi$ is proper, each $Y_i$ is
281	an irreducible closed subvariety of $\overline S'$. Moreover, $Y_i
282	\subset \widetilde\pi(\widetilde{\cA}\ssm \cA) \subset \overline S'\ssm S$. So $Y_i$ has
283	dimension at most $\dim \overline S' - 1$. But $Y_i$ could have
284	codimension at least $2$ and thus fail to be the support of a Weil divisor.
285	On the regular $\overline S'$ a 
286	Cartier divisor is the same thing as a Weil divisor;
287	see \cite[Theorem~11.38(2)]{GoertzWedhorn}.
288	For each $i$ we fix a Cartier divisor  $E_i$ of $\overline S'$
289	such that   $\mathrm{cyc}(E_i)$ equals a prime Weil divisor supported on an irreducible
290	subvariety containing $Y_i$.
291	 Since $\mathrm{cyc}(E_i)$ is effective, we find that $E_i$ is effective, see \cite[Theorem~11.38(1)]{GoertzWedhorn}
292	 and its proof. 
293	An effective Cartier divisor and its image under the cycle map
294	$\mathrm{cyc}(\cdot)$ have equal support. So the subscheme of $\overline S'$ attached to  $E_i$
295	contains $Y_i$. 
296	
297	The pull-back $\widetilde\pi^*E_i$ is well-defined as a Cartier
298	divisor, we do not require that $\pi$ is flat, \textit{cf.}
299	\cite[Proposition~11.48(b)]{GoertzWedhorn}. 
300	By \cite[Corollary~11.49]{GoertzWedhorn} the inverse image
301	$\widetilde\pi^{-1}(E_i)$, taken as  a subscheme of $\widetilde{\cA}$
302	is the subscheme attached to  $\widetilde\pi^*E_i$ and $\widetilde\pi^*E_i$ is
303	effective. 
304	
305	Note that $\widetilde\pi^{-1}(E_i)\supset \widetilde\pi^{-1}(Y_i)\supset Z_i$. 
306	The support satisfies $\mathrm{Supp}(\widetilde\pi^* E_i)\supset Z_i$.
307	Moreover, as $\widetilde\pi^*E_i$ is effective,  $\mathrm{cyc}(\widetilde\pi^* E_i)$ is effective and
308	 $\mathrm{Supp} (\mathrm{cyc}(\widetilde\pi^* E_i)) =
309	\mathrm{Supp}(\widetilde\pi^*E_i)$.
310	%% The latter requires Exercise 11.16 in GW, no regularity
311	%% condition on \widetilde\cA is needed.
312	Thus
313	%$\mathrm{cyc}(\pi^* E_i) \ge Z_i$. Then
314	\begin{equation*}
315	\pm   \sum_{i=1}^r n_i Z_i \le \mathrm{cyc}\left(\widetilde\pi^*\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i|
316	  E_i\right).
317	\end{equation*}
318	
319	Recall that $\mathrm{cyc}(D) = \mathrm{cyc}(\mathrm{div}\phi) +
320	\sum_{i=1}^r n_i Z_i$ for some rational function $\phi$ on
321	$\widetilde{\cA}$. Therefore,
322	%% \begin{equation*}
323	%%   \pm \mathrm{cyc}(D - \mathrm{div}(f)) \le
324	%%   \mathrm{cyc}\left(\widetilde\pi^*\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i| E_i\right).
325	%% \end{equation*} 
326	% Thus the support of $\mathrm{cyc}(D-\mathrm{div}(f))$ is contained in
327	% the support of $  \mathrm{cyc}\left(\pi^*\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i|
328	%   E_i\right)$. 
329	%% Thus
330	\begin{equation*}
331	%  \pm \mathrm{cyc}(D - \mathrm{div}(f)) \le
332	0\le   \mathrm{cyc}\left(\pm (D-\mathrm{div}\phi) + \widetilde\pi^*\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i| E_i\right).
333	\end{equation*}
334	
335	Since $\widetilde{\cA}$ is regular and in particular normal, we find  that 
336	\begin{equation}
337	\label{eq:thisiseffective}\pm(D - \mathrm{div}\phi) + \widetilde\pi^*\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i|E_i
338	\end{equation}
339	is an effective Cartier divisor for both  signs;
340	see \cite[Theorem~11.38(1)]{GoertzWedhorn} and its proof.
341	%% \footnote{I think this is
342	%%   the only point where we require some regularity property of
343	%%   $\widetilde\cA$. So perhaps it suffices to normalize $\overline{\overline\cA}$.}
344	Moreover, its support equals the support of 
345	$$0\le \mathrm{cyc}\left(\pm (D-\mathrm{div}\phi) + \widetilde\pi^*\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i|
346	  E_i\right) =
347	\pm \mathrm{cyc}(D-\mathrm{div}\phi) + \sum_{i=1}^r |n_i|\mathrm{cyc}(\widetilde\pi^*E_i).$$
348	Thus the support of (\ref{eq:thisiseffective}) lies in
349	$\bigcup_{i=1}^r \mathrm{Supp}(\widetilde\pi^* E_i)$. 
350	
351	
352	We apply $\cO(\cdot)$ and pass  again  to line bundles.
353	Let us denote $\mathcal{E} = \mathcal{O}(\sum_{i=1}^r |n_i| E_i)$, a
354	line bundle on $\overline S'$. The line bundle attached to 
355	 (\ref{eq:thisiseffective}) is $(\mathcal{F}\otimes\beta^*\overline\pi^*\mathcal{M}^{\otimes(-1)} 
356	 )^{\otimes(\pm 1)}\otimes\widetilde\pi^*\mathcal{E}$.
357	 Since (\ref{eq:thisiseffective}) is effective, both
358	$(\mathcal{F}\otimes\beta^*\overline\pi^*\mathcal{M}^{\otimes(-1)} 
359	 )^{\otimes(\pm 1)}\otimes \widetilde\pi^*\mathcal{E}$
360	% $\pi^*(\cE\otimes\cM^{\otimes (\mp 1)})\otimes\cF^{\otimes(\pm 1)}$ 
361	have a non-zero global
362	 section.
363	
364	By the Height Machine this translates to 
365	 \begin{equation*}
366	   h_{\widetilde \cA,(\mathcal{F}\otimes\beta^*\overline\pi^*\mathcal{M}^{\otimes(-1)} 
367	 )^{\otimes(\pm 1)}\otimes \widetilde\pi^*\mathcal{E}}(P)\ge O(1)
368	 \end{equation*}
369	 for all $\widetilde P\in \widetilde\cA(\IQbar)$ 
370	with $\widetilde\pi(\widetilde P)\not\in\bigcup_i \mathrm{Supp}(E_i)$. 
371	% where $U = \widetilde\cA\ssm \bigcup_i
372	 % \mathrm{Supp}(\pi^* E_i)$. 
373	By functoriality properties of  the Height Machine we obtain 
374	 \begin{equation*}
375	  |h_{\overline \cA',\cF'}(f(\beta(\widetilde P)))| \le h_{\overline S',\cE}(\widetilde\pi(\widetilde P)) + |h_{\overline
376	    S', \cM}(\overline{\pi}(\beta(\widetilde P)))|
377	  +O(1)
378	 \end{equation*}
379	 for the same $\widetilde P$. 
380	 We recall (\ref{def:cF2}) and again use the Height Machine to find
381	 \begin{equation*}
382	\left|   \hcA([2](P')) - 4
383	   \hcA(\rho(P'))\right|   \le h_{\overline
384	   S',\cE}(\widetilde\pi(\widetilde P)) + |h_{\overline S',
385	     \cM}(\widetilde\pi(\widetilde P))|
386	  +O(1)
387	 \end{equation*}
388	where $P'=f(\beta(\widetilde P))$. Observe that all points of $\cA(\IQbar)$ are
389	in the image of $f\circ \beta$. 
390	
391	We recall that the desingularization morphism $\overline S'\rightarrow\overline S$ is an
392	isomorphism above $S$ and that we have identified $\cA$ with a Zariski
393	open subset of $\overline{\cA}'$ and of $\overline{\cA}$.
394	Under these identifications and if $P'$ corresponds to $P\in
395	\cA(\IQbar)$, then $[2](P')$ is the duplicate of $P$,
396	$\rho(P')= P$, and $\widetilde\pi(\widetilde P) = \pi(\rho(P'))=\pi(P)$.  We apply the Height
397	 Machine a final time and use that $h_{\overline S}$ arises from the
398	 Weil height restricted to $\overline{S}(\IQbar)$. We
399	 find 
400	 \begin{equation*}
401	%\label{eq:preliminary1}
402	\left|   \hcA([2](P)) - 4
403	   \hcA(P)\right|   \le c_1
404	 \max\{1,h_{\overline S}(\pi(P))\}
405	 \end{equation*}
406	 for all $P\in \cA(\IQbar)$   with  $\widetilde\pi(\widetilde
407	 P)\not\in \bigcup_i \mathrm{Supp}(E_i)$, under the identifications
408	 above. 
409	
410	Let $P\in\cA(\IQbar)$. As the $Y_i$ lie in $\widetilde\pi(\widetilde\cA\ssm
411	\cA)$ we can choose all $E_i$ above to avoid $\pi(P)$.  After doing this finitely often (using
412	noetherian induction) and replacing the $E_i$ from before and
413	adjusting $c_1$, we find 
414	\begin{equation*}
415	\left|   \hcA([2](P)) - 4
416	   \hcA(P)\right|  \le c_1 \max\{1,h_{\overline S}(\pi(P))\}
417	\end{equation*}
418	for all $P\in \cA(\IQbar)$ where $c_1>0$ is independent of $P$.
419	\end{proof}
420	
421	\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:silvermantate}]
422	  Having (\ref{eq:duplicationbound}) at our disposal the proof follows
423	  a well-known argument.   
424	  Indeed, say $l\ge k\ge 0$ are integers.
425	  Then applying the triangle inequality to the appropriate telescoping
426	  sum yields
427	  \begin{alignat*}1
428	    \left|\frac{\hcA([2^l](P))}{4^l}- \frac{\hcA([2^k](P))}{4^k}\right|
429	    &\le \sum_{m=k}^{l-1}
430	        \left|\frac{\hcA([2^{m+1}](P))}{4^{m+1}}-
431	        \frac{\hcA([2^m](P))}{4^m}\right|\\
432	        &\le \sum_{m=k}^{l-1}
433	        4^{-(m+1)}    \left|{\hcA([2^{m+1}](P))}- 4{\hcA([2^m](P))}\right|.
434	  \end{alignat*}
435	  We apply (\ref{eq:duplicationbound}) to $[2^m](P)$ and find that the
436	  sum is bounded by $c_1 x \sum_{m=k}^{l-1}4^{-(m+1)} \le c_1 x 4^{-k}$
437	  where $x = \max\{1,h_{\overline S}(\pi(P))\}$.
438	  So $\left(\hcA([2^l](P))/4^l\right)_{l\ge 1}$ is a Cauchy sequence
439	  with limit
440	  $\hat h_{\overline\cA}(P)$. 
441	  Taking $k=0$ and $l\rightarrow \infty$ we obtain from the estimates
442	  above that
443	  $|\hat h_{\overline\cA}(P) - \hcA(P)|\le c_1x$, as
444	  desired.   
445	\end{proof}
