2	
3	We keep the notation of $\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}. 
4	So we have a closed immersion $\cA\rightarrow \IP_k^n\times
5	S$ over $S$ satisfying the properties stated near the beginning 
6	of $\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}. Moreover, $S$ is a
7	Zariski open subset of an irreducible projective variety $\overline
8	S\subset\IP_k^m$. 
9	We assume in addition $k = \IQbar$. Let $X$ be a closed irreducible subvariety of $\cA$ of dimension $d$ defined over $\IQbar$, such that $\pi|_X \colon X\rightarrow S$ is dominant. Let $\omega$ be the Betti form on $\cA$ as defined in Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}.
10	\begin{proposition}\label{PropAuxHtIneq}
11	We keep the notation from above and
12	suppose that $\an{X}$ contains a smooth point at which
13	$\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d} >0$. Then there exists a constant $c_1
14	> 0$ satisfying the following property.
15	Let $N \in \IN$ be a power of $2$, there exist a Zariski open dense subset $U_N$ of $X$ defined over $\IQbar$ and a constant $c_2(N)$ such that
16	\[
17	h([N]P) \ge c_1 N^2 h(\pi(P)) - c_2(N)\qquad \text{for all }P \in U_N(\IQbar).
18	\]
19	\end{proposition}
20	
21	The goal of this section is to prove Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq}. 
22	The key idea is to apply a theorem of Siu \cite[Theorem~2.2.15]{PosAlgGeom}. Let us briefly explain the main points before moving on to the proof.
23	
24	Let $X$ be as in Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq}, and let $P \in
25	X(\IQbar)$. For each $N \in \IN$, we work with $X_N \subseteq
26	\cA\times_S \cA$, the graph of $[N] \colon \cA \rightarrow \cA$, and
27	its Zariski closure $\overline{X_N}$ in
28	$\overline{\cA}\times_{\overline{S}}\overline{\cA} \subseteq \IP_k^n
29	\times \IP_k^n \times \IP_k^m$. The point $P$ gives rise to a point
30	$P_N \in \overline{X_N}$; see $\mathsection$\ref{ss:graph}. Consider
31	the line bundles $\cF = \cO(0,1,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$ and $\cM =
32	\cO(0,0,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$. Choosing representatives as in
33	last paragraph of
34	$\mathsection$\ref{ss:graph}  
35	 our height inequality in Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq} is equivalent to
36	\[
37	h_{\overline{X_N},\cF}(P_N)\ge c_1 N^2 h_{\overline{X_N},\cM}(P_N) - c_2'(N)
38	\]
39	for some $c_2'(N)$ independent of $P$ (which may be different from
40	$c_2(N)$). By the Height Machine  it suffices to find positive
41	integers $p$ and $q$, independent of $N$, such that $\cF^{\otimes q}
42	\otimes \cM^{\otimes -p N^2}$ is a big line bundle on
43	$\overline{X_N}$; we can then take $c_1=p/q$. 
44	
45	Both $\cF$ and $\cM$ are nef line bundles. Thus a criterion of bigness
46	by Siu \cite[Theorem~2.2.15]{PosAlgGeom}, states that $\cF^{\otimes q}
47	\otimes \cM^{\otimes -p N^2}$ is big if $(\cF^{\cdot d}) > d c_1
48	(\cM^{\otimes N^2} \cdot \cF^{\cdot (d-1)})$.
49	Note that $(\cM^{\otimes N^2} \cdot \cF^{\cdot (d-1)}) = N^2 (\cM \cdot \cF^{\cdot (d-1)})$ by multi-linearity of intersection numbers. 
50	Thus our task 
51	becomes comparing two intersection numbers. Our application continues
52	to work if the numerical
53	factor $d = \dim X$ is replaced by any
54	positive  factor that depends only on the dimension.
55	So it remains to prove an appropriate lower bound for $(\cF^{\cdot d})$ and an appropriate upper bound for $(\cM \cdot \cF^{\cdot (d-1)})$.
56	
57	The proof of Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq} will be organized as follows in this section. We first prove the appropriate lower bound for $(\cF^{\cdot d})$ in Proposition~\ref{prop:intersectionlb}. This is where we use the hypothesis that $\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d} >0$ at some smooth point of $\an{X}$. Next we prove the appropriate lower bound for $(\cM \cdot \cF^{\cdot (d-1)})$ in Proposition~\ref{prop:intersectionub}. At this step the assumption of $N$ being a power of $2$ is used. Then we finish the proof of Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq} by applying Siu's theorem in $\mathsection$\ref{SubsectionProofOfAUxHtIneq}.
58

62	\subsection{Bounding an intersection number from below}
63	Let $X$ be as in Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq}. 
64	For each $N \in \IN$, let $\overline{X_N} \subseteq \IP_k^n \times
65	\IP_k^n \times \IP_k^m$ be as in $\mathsection$\ref{ss:graph}.
66	In particular, $\dim \overline{X_N} = d$.
67	%Without loss of generality we may assume $k=\IC$. 
68	 Let $\cF =
69	\cO(0,1,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$ be as in \eqref{def:cF}. The top
70	self-intersection of $\cF$ on $\overline{X_N}$ is bounded from below
71	in the following proposition.
72	To prove it, we may replace $X$ by its base change to $\IC$. 
73	
74	\begin{proposition}
75	  \label{prop:intersectionlb}
76	  Suppose $\an{X}$ contains a smooth point at which
77	  $\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d} >0$. Then there exists a constant $\kappa
78	  > 0$, independent of $N$, such that $(\cF^{\cdot d}) \ge \kappa N^{2d}$ for all
79	  $N\in\IN$.   
80	\end{proposition}
81	\begin{proof}
82	We fix a point  $P_0 \in \sman{X}$ at which
83	$\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}$ is positive and let $s_0=\pi(P_0),\Delta,\vartheta,\theta,$
84	and $K$  be as in $\mathsection$\ref{SubsectionSetUpHtIneqBetti}, see 
85	Remark~\ref{rmk:nondeg}. In particular $\vartheta(P_0) = \theta\circ \pi(P_0) = 1$. 
86	We extend $\vartheta$ to a smooth function on  $\an{(\IP^m_\IC)}$ by
87	setting it $0$ outside of the compact set $K\subset \an{S}$. This
88	extends $\theta = \vartheta\circ\pi$ to all of
89	$\an{(\IP^n_\IC\times\IP^m_\IC)}$. 
90	  %%   As stated above, $\int_{\sman{X}} \omega^{\wedge d}$ is not
91	%% well-defined if
92	%% $\omega$ does not have compact support. However, $\omega_c$ as in
93	%% (\ref{def:omegac}) does  have
94	%% compact support on $\cA$. 
95	
96	Let $\alpha$ be the pull-back of the Fubini--Study form 
97	 under the analytification of the Segre morphism
98	$\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^m \rightarrow \IP_{\IC}^{(n+1)(m+1)-1}$.
99	We replace $\alpha$ by its restriction to $\an{\overline\cA}$. 
100	Thus $\alpha$
101	represents the Chern class of
102	$\cO(1,1) \in \mathrm{Pic}(\IP^n_{\IC}\times\IP_{\IC}^m)$ restricted
103	 to $\an{\overline\cA}$, using common notation.
104	
105	%% Recall that  $\alpha$ represents the Chern class of an ample line
106	%% bundle on ${\cA}$. So it is
107	Note that $\alpha$ is strictly positive on all of
108	$\an{\cA}$.  Since $\Delta$ is relatively compact we can find a constant $C>0$
109	with
110	\begin{equation}\label{EqConditionOnCapitalC}
111	 C \alpha|_{\cA_{\Delta}} - \omega|_{\cA_{\Delta}} \ge 0.
112	\end{equation}
113	As the smooth and non-negative function $\theta = \vartheta\circ\pi$ on $\an{\cA}$ has support in
114	$\pi^{-1}(K)\subset \pi^{-1}(\Delta)=\cA_{\Delta}$ we have
115	\begin{equation*}
116	  C \theta\alpha - \theta\omega \ge 0.
117	\end{equation*}
118	
119	We pull this $(1,1)$-form back under the holomorphic map
120	$[N]\colon \an{\cA}\rightarrow\an{\cA}$ and get
121	\begin{equation}
122	\label{eq:pbsemipositive}
123	C  [N]^* (\theta\alpha) - N^2\theta\omega =  C [N]^*(\theta\alpha) - [N]^*(\theta\omega)\ge 0
124	\end{equation}
125	where we used $[N]^*\omega = N^2\omega$ and $[N]^* \theta = \theta$; 
126	the former is a property of the Betti form, see
127	Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}(ii) and
128	the latter holds as $\theta$ is the pull-back from the base of $\vartheta$.
129	
130	We define
131	\begin{equation*}
132	  \beta = C  [N]^*(\theta\alpha) - N^2\theta\omega,
133	\end{equation*}
134	which is a  $(1,1)$-form on $\an{\cA}$.
135	It is semi-positive by (\ref{eq:pbsemipositive}). 
136	The support of $\theta$ is
137	contained in $\pi^{-1}(K)$, which we have identified as compact at the
138	end of $\mathsection$\ref{SubsectionSetUpHtIneqBetti}.
139	So
140	$C[N]^*(\theta\alpha)$ and $N^2\theta\omega$ have compact support on $\an{\cA}$. % Thus we are allowed to integrate.
141	
142	%\begin{lemma}
143	We claim that  $\int_{\sman{X}} (C[N]^*(\theta\alpha))^{\wedge d} \ge
144	\int_{\sman{X}} (N^2 \theta\omega)^{\wedge d}$.
145	
146	First observe that both integrals are well-defined as both
147	$[N]^*(\theta\alpha)$ and $N^2\theta\omega$ have compact support on
148	$\an{\cA}$; this follows from work of Lelong~\cite{Lelong} which we
149	use freely below. A textbook proof can be found in
150	\cite[Theorem~11.21]{voisin:hodge1} and
151	\cite[$\mathsection$III.2.B]{Demailly}. 
152	%\end{lemma}
153	%\begin{proof}
154	To prove the inequality let us  write $\beta = \gamma - \delta$ with $\gamma = C[N]^*(\theta
155	\alpha)$ and $\delta = N^2\theta\omega$.
156	% all of compact support, [PH]removed, as redundant
157	Then
158	\begin{equation}
159	\label{eq:diffintegral}  
160	  \int_{\sman{X}} \gamma^{\wedge d} - \int_{\sman{X}}\delta^{\wedge d}
161	  = 
162	\int_{\sman{X}} (\delta+\beta)^{\wedge d} - \int_{\sman{X}} \delta^{\wedge
163	    d}
164	= \sum_{i=0}^{d-1} {d \choose i} \int_{\sman{X}} \delta^{\wedge
165	  i}\wedge \beta^{\wedge (d-i)}
166	\end{equation}
167	as the exterior product is commutative on even degree forms. 
168	We know that $\beta \ge 0$ on $\an{\cA}$ and it is also crucial that
169	$\delta \ge 0$ on $\an{\cA}$, the latter
170	follows from $\omega \ge 0$, property (i) of
171	Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm},
172	and from $\theta\ge 0$.
173	Then $\delta^{\wedge i}\wedge \beta^{\wedge (d-i)}$ is semi-positive
174	on $\an{\cA}$; see \cite[Proposition~III.1.11]{Demailly}\footnote{As our convention  is somewhat different from Demailly's, let us explain how to apply  \cite[Proposition~III.1.11]{Demailly}. Our definition of semi-positive $(1,1)$-form coincides with that of \textit{positive $(1,1)$-form} of \cite[Chapter~III]{Demailly} by Corollary~1.7 of \textit{loc.cit.}, and thus are precisely the strongly positive $(1,1)$-forms of \cite[Chapter~III]{Demailly} by Corollary~1.9 of \textit{loc.cit.} Therefore we can apply the cited proposition.}. Thus the right-hand side of (\ref{eq:diffintegral}) is
175	 non-negative, see \cite[Theorem~III.2.7]{Demailly}, and our claim is settled.
176	%\end{proof}
177	
178	The claim implies
179	\begin{equation}
180	\label{eq:N2dgrowth}
181	 C^d \int_{\sman{X}}  [N]^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d} \ge
182	\kappa' N^{2d} \quad\text{where}\quad \kappa' = \int_{\sman{X}} (\theta\omega)^{\wedge d}.
183	\end{equation}
184	We have $\kappa' > 0$. Indeed, $(\theta\omega)^{\wedge d}$ is semi-positive on $\an{\cA}$ because $\omega \ge 0$ (Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm}(i)) and $\theta \ge 0$ (by construction). But $\omega|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}$ is positive at $P_0 \in \sman{X}$ by choice of $P_0$ and $\theta\circ \pi(P_0) = 1$ by choice of $\theta$. So $(\theta\omega)|_{\sman{X}}^{\wedge d}$ is positive at $P_0 \in \sman{X}$. Thus $\kappa' > 0$.
185	
186	Next we want to relate the integral on the left in (\ref{eq:N2dgrowth}) with an
187	intersection number. First we recall that $[N]$ is given in terms of
188	the graph construction, \textit{cf.} (\ref{eq:relatetoN}). So we may
189	rewrite
190	\begin{equation}
191	\label{eq:chainbegins}
192	  \int_{\sman{X}} [N]^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}  =
193	  \int_{\sman{X}} (\rho_2|_{\Gamma_N} \circ
194	  \rho_1|_{\Gamma_N}^{-1})^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}=
195	  \int_{\sman{X}} (\rho_1|_{\Gamma_N}^{-1})^*\rho_2|_{\Gamma_N}^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d},
196	\end{equation}
197	here $\Gamma_N,\rho_1,$ and $\rho_2$ are as defined in $\mathsection$\ref{ss:graph}. 
198	
199	Because $\rho_1|_{\an{\Gamma}_N} \colon \an{\Gamma}_N \rightarrow \an{\cA}$ is biholomorphic we
200	can change coordinates and integrate over $X_N$, which is a complex
201	analytic subset of the graph $\Gamma_N$, itself a complex manifold.
202	More precisely, we have
203	\begin{equation}
204	\label{eq:chain2} 
205	  \int_{\sman{X}} (\rho_1|_{\Gamma_N}^{-1})^*\rho_2|_{\Gamma_N}^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}
206	=\int_{\sman{X}_N}  \rho_2|_{\Gamma_N}^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}.
207	\end{equation} 
208	
209	
210	Recall that $\alpha$ is the restriction to $\an{\overline{\cA}}$ 
211	of a  $(1,1)$-form on  
212	$\an{(\IP^n_{\IC}\times\IP^m_{\IC})}$.
213	Moreover,
214	$\rho_2$ is also defined on all of $\IP^n_{\IC} \times\IP^n_{\IC}\times \IP^m_{\IC}$. So ${\rho_2}|_{\Gamma_N}^*(\theta\alpha)$ is the restriction to
215	$\Gamma_N$ of a $(1,1)$-form defined on $\an{(\IP^n_{\IC} \times\IP^n_{\IC}\times \IP^m_{\IC})}$. 
216	Observe that $\sman{X}_N\subset \an{\overline{X_N}}$ and the difference has
217	dimension strictly less than $d=\dim X_N$.
218	This justifies% the first inequality in 
219	\begin{equation}
220	\label{eq:chain3}   
221	  \int_{\sman{X}_N} \rho_2|_{\Gamma_N}^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}
222	  =\int_{\an{\overline{X_N}}} \rho_2^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}
223	%  \le \int_{\an{\overline{X_N}}} \rho_2^*\alpha^{\wedge d}
224	\end{equation}
225	where we take $\an{\overline{X_N}}$ as a complex analytic subset of
226	the analytification of $\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^n\times \IP_{\IC}^m$ and $\rho_2^*(\theta\alpha)$ as a
227	$(1,1)$-form on this ambient space.
228	Now $\theta$ takes values in $[0,1]$ and so
229	\begin{equation}
230	\label{eq:chain4}
231	\int_{\an{\overline{X_N}}} \rho_2^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d}\le
232	%  \int_{\an{\overline{X_N}}} \theta^d(\rho_2^*\alpha)^{\wedge d}\le
233	  \int_{\an{\overline{X_N}}} (\rho_2^*\alpha)^{\wedge d}.
234	\end{equation}
235	
236	
237	The pull-back $\rho_2^*\alpha$ represents
238	$\rho_2^* \cO(1,1) \in
239	\mathrm{Pic}(\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^m)$ in the
240	Picard group and
241	has compact support as $\an{(\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^n\times \IP_{\IC}^m)}$ is
242	compact.
243	But integration coincides with the intersection pairing in the compact
244	case; see \cite[Theorem~11.21]{voisin:hodge1}. In particular, we have
245	\begin{equation}
246	\label{eq:chain5} 
247	\int_{\an{\overline{X_N}}} (\rho_2^*\alpha)^{\wedge d} = (\rho_2^*
248	\cO(1,1)^{\cdot d} [\overline {X_N}]) %= \left(\cO(1,1,0)^{\cdot d}[\overline {X_N}]\right)
249	\end{equation}
250	where the intersection takes place in
251	$\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^n\times\IP_{\IC}^m$. We recall \eqref{def:cF}
252	and apply  the projection formula to obtain 
253	\begin{equation}
254	\label{eq:chain6}  
255	  (\rho_2^*
256	\cO(1,1)^{\cdot d}  [\overline {X_N}]) = (\cO(0,1,1)^{\cdot d}  [\overline {X_N}]) =  (\cF^{\cdot d}).
257	\end{equation}
258	%where we use $(\cF^{\cdot d})$ to denote  the self-intersection number on $\overline{X_N}$. 
259	
260	The (in)equalities  (\ref{eq:chainbegins}),
261	(\ref{eq:chain2}), (\ref{eq:chain3}),
262	(\ref{eq:chain4}), (\ref{eq:chain5}), and (\ref{eq:chain6}) yield
263	\begin{equation*}
264	  \int_{\sman{X}} [N]^*(\theta\alpha)^{\wedge d} \le (\cF^{\cdot d}). 
265	\end{equation*}
266	We recall the  lower bound (\ref{eq:N2dgrowth}) % and the definition
267	% (\ref{def:cF})
268	to  obtain
269	$(\cF^{\cdot d}) \ge (\kappa'/C^d) N^{2d}$ where $C$ comes from
270	\eqref{EqConditionOnCapitalC} and $\kappa' > 0$ comes from
271	\eqref{eq:N2dgrowth}.
272	The proposition follows with $\kappa = \kappa'/C^d$. 
273	\end{proof}

276	\subsection{Bounding an intersection number from above}
277	
278	We keep the notation from the last subsection with $k=\IQbar$. 
279	So  $X$ is as above Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq} with $\dim X=d$
280	For each $N \in \IN$, let $\overline{X_N} \subseteq \IP_k^n \times
281	\IP_k^n \times \IP_k^m$ be the graph construction as in $\mathsection$\ref{ss:graph}. In
282	particular, $\dim \overline{X_N} = d$.  Here we need $\cF =
283	\cO(0,1,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$ as defined in (\ref{def:cF}) and also
284	 $\cM = \cO(0,0,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$ as defined in \eqref{def:cM}.
285	
286	\begin{proposition}
287	  \label{prop:intersectionub}
288	Assume $d \ge 1$.  There exists a constant $c>0$ depending on the data introduced above
289	  with the following property. Say $N\ge 1$ is a power of $2$, then
290	  \begin{equation*}
291	    (\cM\cdot \cF^{\cdot(d-1)}) \le c N^{2(d-1)}.
292	  \end{equation*}
293	% where  $\cM =\rho_0|_{\overline{X_N}}^*\cO(1)$.
294	\end{proposition}
295	
296	Let us make some preliminary remarks before the proof. A similar upper
297	bound for the intersection number was derived by the third-named
298	author in \cite{habegger:imrn,Hab:Special} using Philippon's
299	version~\cite{Philippon} of B\'ezout's Theorem for multiprojective
300	space. The approach here is similar but does not
301	 refer to Philippon's result. Rather, we rely on the following well-known
302	positivity property of the intersection theory of multiprojective
303	space: any effective Weil divisor on a multiprojective space is
304	nef. This approach was motivated by
305	K\"uhne's~\cite{kuehne:semiabelianbhc} work on semiabelian varieties.
306	% We refer to Appendix~\ref{app:positivity} for
307	%  the intersection theory we need.
308	
309	\begin{proof}[Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:intersectionub}] Recall that $[2] \colon
310	A \rightarrow A$  is the  multiplication-by-$2$ morphism on $A$. For the symmetric and ample line bundle
311	$L$ on $A$, we have $[2]^*L \cong L^{\otimes 4}$.
312	Recall that $A$ is projectively normal in $\IP_{k(S)}^n$. 
313	By a result of Serre, \cite[Corollaire~2, Appendix~II]{Ast6970}, 
314	the morphism $[2]$ is represented 
315	 by homogeneous
316	polynomials $f_0,\ldots,f_n$ in the $n+1$ projective coordinates of $\IP^n$ of degree $4$, with  coefficients in $k(S)$ and with no common
317	zeros in $A$. 
318	
319	Recall that the family $\cA$ is embedded in
320	$\IP_k^n\times S \subset\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m$. We can spread out the
321	$f_0,\ldots,f_n$. More precisely, there exist a Zariski closed, proper
322	subset $Z\subsetneq \cA$ and polynomials $f_0,\ldots,f_n \in
323	k[\mathbf{X},\mathbf{S}]$ that are bihomogeneous of degree $(4,D')$ in
324	 the $(n+1)$-tuple of projective coordinates $\mathbf{X}$ of
325	$\IP_k^n$
326	and the $(m+1)$-tuple of projective coordinates $\mathbf{S}$ of
327	$\IP_k^m$,
328	 with the following properties:
329	\begin{enumerate}
330	 \item [(i)] the polynomials $f_0,\ldots,f_n$ have no common zeros on
331	 $(\cA\setminus Z)(k)$, and 
332	 \item[(ii)]  if   $(P,s)\in(\cA\setminus Z)(k)$, then $[2](P,s) = \bigl([f_0(P,s):\cdots : f_n(P,s)],s\bigr)$. 
333	\end{enumerate}
334	Moreover, as $f_0,\ldots,f_n$ have no common zero on the generic
335	fiber, we may assume that $\pi(Z)$ is Zariski closed and proper in
336	$S$. So we may assume that $Z = \pi^{-1}(\pi(Z))\subsetneq \cA$ and in particular,
337	$[2]$ maps $\cA\setminus Z$ to itself.
338	
339	The  $4$ in the bidegree $(4,D')$ comes from $2^2=4$. The degree
340	$D'$ with respect to the base coordinates $\mathbf{S}$ is more mysterious. However,
341	by successively iterating we will get it under control. 
342	
343	%% \begin{remark}
344	%% This degree may be a source of ineffectivity! But it is probably
345	%% computable for $\mathcal{A}_g$.   
346	%% \end{remark}
347	
348	For each integer $l\ge 1$ we require polynomials
349	$f^{(l)}_0,\ldots,f^{(l)}_n$ to describe multiplication-by-$2^l$,
350	\textit{cf.} \cite[$\mathsection$9]{GaoHab}. In order to obtain information on the
351	degree with respect to $\mathbf{S}$ we construct them by iterating
352	the $f^{(1)}_0=f_0,\ldots,f^{(1)}_n=f_n$.
353	For all $i\in \{0,\ldots,n\}$  we set
354	\begin{equation*}
355	%  f^{(l+1)}_i((S_j),(X_j)) =
356	%  f\left((S_j)_j,\left(f^{(l)}_0((S_j)_j,(X_j)_j),\ldots,f^{(l)}_n((S_j)_j,(X_j)_j)\right)\right)
357	  f^{(l+1)}_i(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{S}) = f_i\left(\bigl(f^{(l)}_0(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{S}),\ldots,f^{(l)}_n(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{S})\bigr),\mathbf{S}\right)
358	\end{equation*}
359	 for all $i$; it is bihomogeneous in
360	$\mathbf{X}$ and $\mathbf{S}$. 
361	So for all $l\ge 1$ 
362	\begin{enumerate}
363	 \item [(i)] the polynomials $f^{(l)}_0,\ldots,f^{(l)}_n$ have no common zeros on
364	 $(\cA\setminus Z)(k)$, and 
365	 \item[(ii)]  if   $(P,s)\in(\cA\setminus Z)(k)$, then $[2^l](P,s) = \bigl([f^{(l)}_0(P,s):\cdots : f^{(l)}_n(P,s)],s\bigr)$. 
366	\end{enumerate}
367	
368	
369	If for all $i$ the polynomials $f^{(l)}_i$ are bihomogeneous of degree
370	$(D_{l},D'_{l})$, then all $f^{(l+1)}_i$ are bihomogeneous of degree
371	$(4D_{l},D'+4D'_{l})$. Recall that
372	$(D_1,D'_1)=(4,D')$, thus the recurrence
373	relations
374	$$ D_{l+1}=4D_l  \quad\text{and}\quad D'_{l+1} = D' + 4D'_{l} $$
375	imply
376	\begin{equation}
377	  \label{eq:DlprimeDlbound}
378	  D_l = 4^l \quad\text{and}\quad   D'_l = \frac{4^l-1}{3}D'\le 4^lD'
379	\end{equation}
380	for all $l\ge 1$.
381	Up-to the constant linear factor $D'$ the bidegrees both
382	grow like $4^l$.
383	
384	
385	We proceed as follows to cut out the graph $\overline{X_{N}}$ where
386	$N=2^l$. 
387	We start out with $\overline X\subset\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m$. 
388	As $X$ dominates $S$ but $Z$ does not, there is an $i$ such that 
389	$f_i^{(l)}$  does not
390	vanish identically on $X$, without loss of generality we assume $i=0$. 
391	
392	
393	Then as $i$ varies over $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ we obtain $n$ trihomogeneous
394	polynomials 
395	\begin{equation*}
396	g_i :=  Y_i f_0^{(l)}(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{S}) - Y_0   f_i^{(l)}(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{S})
397	\end{equation*}
398	where $Y_0,\ldots,Y_n$ are the projective coordinates on the middle factor of $\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m$. 
399	The tridegree of these polynomials is $(D_l,1,D'_l)$. %\Pcomment{There
400	  %was a mistake here, $(D'_l,1,D_l)$ instead of $(D_l,1,D'_l)$.}
401	Their zero locus on $\overline X\times\IP_k^n$ has the graph
402	$\overline{X_N}$ as an irreducible component;
403	by permuting coordinates we consider $\overline X\times\IP_k^n$ as a
404	subvariety of $\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m$.
405	We will see below that this is a proper component of the said
406	intersection. 
407	However, there may be further irreducible components in this
408	intersection,  some could even have dimension greater than $\dim
409	\overline{X_N}$.
410	
411	%Observe that $\dim\overline{X_N} =\dim\overline X = d$. 
412	This issue is clarified by the positivity result \cite[Corollary
413	12.2.(a)]{Fulton}. We apply it to the ambient
414	variety 
415	$\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m$, which becomes $X$ in Fulton's
416	notation; observe that
417	 the tangent bundle of a product of projective spaces
418	is generated by its global sections, \textit{cf.}~\cite[Examples 12.2.1.(a) and
419	(c)]{Fulton}. For $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$, the $V_i$ in Fulton's notation
420	is the zero set of $g_i$, and $V_{n+1}$ is 
421	$\overline{X} \times \IP_k^n$. So $r=n+1$ and $V_1,\ldots,V_{n+1}$ are
422	equidimensional. 
423	Observe that
424	\begin{alignat*}1
425	  \sum_{i=1}^{r} \dim V_i  - (r-1) \dim
426	  \IP_k^n\times\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m
427	  &= (2n+m-1)(r-1) + \dim\overline{X}\times\IP_k^n
428	  - (r-1)(2n+m)
429	  \\
430	  &= \dim \overline{X} = \dim \overline{X_N},
431	\end{alignat*}
432	% Observe that $\dim \overline{X_N} = \dim X
433	% = \dim (\overline{X} \times \IP_k^n) - n$,
434	so, and as announced above, $\overline{X_N}$ is a proper component in the intersection of
435	$V_1,\ldots,V_n,$ and $\overline{X} \times \IP_k^n$.
436	By Fulton's \cite[Corollary
437	12.2.(a)]{Fulton} the  cycle class
438	attached to the intersection of $\overline X\times\IP_k^n$ with the zero
439	locus of $g_1,\ldots,g_n$
440	is represented by a positive cycle on
441	$\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^n\times\IP_k^m$, one of
442	whose components is $\overline{X_N}$. As $\cO(0,0,1)$ and $\cO(0,1,1)$
443	are numerically effective we conclude 
444	\begin{alignat}1
445	  \label{eq:inupperboundconclusion1}
446	  (\cO(0,0,1)  \cO(0,1,1)^{\cdot (d-1)}  [\overline{X_N}])
447	  &\le
448	  \left(\cO(0,0,1)  \cO(0,1,1)^{\cdot (d-1)}
449	  \cO(D_l,1,D'_l)^{\cdot n}  [\overline{X} \times\IP_k^n]\right).
450	%\\
451	 % &\le 4^{l(d-1)}{D'}^{d-1}{n+d\choose n}(\cO(1,1)^{\cdot d}[\overline{X}])
452	\end{alignat}
453	
454	The cycle $[\overline X\times\IP_k^n]$ is linearly equivalent to 
455	$\sum_{i+p=n+m-d} a_{ip}H_1^{\cdot i}  H_2^{\cdot p}$, with $H_{1}$ and $H_2$
456	hyperplane pullbacks of the factors $\IP_k^n\times \IP_k^m\supset
457	\overline X$, respectively, and with
458	$a_{ip}$ non-negative integers that depend only on $\overline X$.
459	Thus the left-hand side of (\ref{eq:inupperboundconclusion1}) is at
460	most %\Pcomment{There was a mistake here, it read $\cO(D'_l,1,D_l)$
461	%  instead of $\cO(D_l,1,D'_l)$.}
462	\begin{alignat*}1
463	\sum_{i+p = n+m-d} a_{ip}
464	\left(  \cO(0,0,1)\cO(0,1,1)^{\cdot (d-1)}
465	\cO(D_l,1,D'_l)^{\cdot n}\cO(1,0,0)^{\cdot i}\cO(0,0,1)^{\cdot p}\right).
466	\end{alignat*}
467	We can expand the sum using linearly of intersection
468	numbers to find that it equals
469	\begin{alignat*}1
470	  \sum_{\substack{i+p=n+m-d\\ j'+p'=d-1\\i''+j''+p'' = n}} a_{ip} {d-1 \choose j',p'}
471	  {n\choose i'',j'',p''}{D_l}^{i''} {D'_l}^{p''} \left(\cO(1,0,0)^{\cdot
472	    (i+i'')} \cO(0,1,0)^{\cdot (j'+j'')}\cO(0,0,1)^{\cdot(1+p+p'+p'')}\right)
473	\end{alignat*}
474	Only terms with $i+i''\le n$ and $j'+j''\le n$ and $1+p+p'+p''\le m$
475	contribute to the sum.
476	On the other hand, any term in the sum satisfies
477	$i+i'' + j'+j'' + 1+p+p'+p'' =2n+m$. 
478	So we can assume $i+i''=n$ and $j'+j''=n$ and $1+p+p'+p''=m$ in the
479	sum
480	which thus simplifies to
481	\begin{alignat*}1
482	  \sum_{\substack{i+p=n+m-d, i+i''=n \\ j'+p'=d-1,j'+j''=n \\ i''+j''+p''=n,p+p'+p''=m-1}} a_{ip} {d-1\choose
483	    j',p'} {n\choose i'',j'',p''} {D_l}^{i''} {D'_l}^{p''}. 
484	\end{alignat*}
485	Note $i''+p''  = n-j'' =j'  = d-1-p'\le d-1$. 
486	We recall (\ref{eq:DlprimeDlbound}) and conclude that the 
487	 left-hand side of (\ref{eq:inupperboundconclusion1}) is at most
488	\begin{equation}
489	  \label{eq:intersectionnumberub2}
490	  (4^lD')^{d-1}
491	  \sum_{\substack{i+p=n+m-d\\ j'+p'=d-1\\i''+j''+p'' = n}}
492	 a_{ip}
493	 {d-1\choose
494	    j',p'} {n\choose i'',j'',p''} 
495	\le
496	 (4^l D')^{d-1}2^{d-1}3^n \sum_{\substack{i+p=n+m-d}}
497	 a_{ip}.
498	\end{equation}
499	%where $c>0$ depends only on $X$. 
500	
501	% $\cO(0,1,1) = \cO(0,1,0)\otimes \cO(0,0,1),
502	% \cO(D'_l,1,D_l) = \cO(1,0,0)^{\otimes D'_l} \otimes\cO(0,1,0)\otimes
503	% \cO(0,0,1)^{D_l}$.  Any occurrence $\cO(1,0,0)^{\cdot i}$ or
504	% $\cO(0,1,0)^{\cdot i}$ with $i>n$ or $\cO(0,0,1)^{\cdot i}$ with $i>m$
505	% does not contribute to the intersection number. 
506	
507	We recall $N=2^l$ and use the projection formula with the estimates
508	above to find 
509	\begin{equation*}
510	  (\cO(0,0,1)|_{\overline{X_N}} 
511	  \cO(0,1,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}^{\cdot(d-1)}) \le  c N^{2(d-1)}
512	\end{equation*}
513	where $c>0$ depends only on $X$. 
514	Recall our definition $\cF = \cO(0,1,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$ and $\cM =
515	\cO(0,0,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}$. So we get 
516	$  (\cM\cdot \cF^{\cdot(d-1)}) \le c N^{2(d-1)}$, as desired. 
517	%Thus we are done by taking $c = {D'}^{d-1}{n+d\choose n}(\cO(1,1)^{\cdot d}[\overline{X}])$.
518	%
519	% We recall $N=2^l$ and use the projection formula and find 
520	% \begin{equation*}
521	%   (\cO(0,0,1)|_{\overline{X_N}} \cdot 
522	%   \cO(0,1,1)|_{\overline{X_N}}^{\cdot(d-1)}) \le  N^{2(d-1)}
523	%       {D'}^{d-1}
524	%       {n+d\choose n}(\cO(1,1)^{\cdot d}[\overline{X}])

538	Now let us prove Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq} by comparing the intersection number inequalities in
539	Propositions~\ref{prop:intersectionlb} and \ref{prop:intersectionub}.
540	
541	Let $X$ be of dimension $d$ as in Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq}. The
542	case $d = 0$ is trivial. So we assume $d \ge 1$. We may assume $N=2^l$
543	with $l \in \IN$. Let $\overline{X_N} \subseteq \IP_k^n \times \IP_k^n \times \IP_k^m$ be as in $\mathsection$\ref{ss:graph}. In particular $\dim \overline{X_N} = d$.
544	
545	Let $\kappa > 0$ be as in Proposition~\ref{prop:intersectionlb}. Then
546	$(\cF^{\cdot d}) \ge \kappa N^{2d}$. Let $c > 0$ be as in
547	Proposition~\ref{prop:intersectionub}. Then $(\cM \cdot \cF^{\cdot
548	(d-1)}) \le c N^{2(d-1)}$.
549	We have indicated how to obtain  $\kappa$ and $c$  at the end of the proof of each one of the corresponding propositions.
550	
551	Fix a rational number $c_1$ such that 
552	\begin{equation}
553	  \label{EqConstantInHtIneq}
554	  0<  c_1  cd < \kappa.
555	\end{equation}
556	Let $q$ be a multiple of the denominator of $c_1$.
557	Using the bounds above and linearity of intersection numbers we get
558	\begin{equation*}
559	d (\cM^{\otimes qc_1 N^2}\cdot(\cF^{\otimes q})^{\cdot(d-1)}) = d q^d
560	c_1 N^2 (\cM \cdot
561	\cF^{\cdot (d-1)}) \le d q^d c_1 N^2 c N^{2(d-1)} < \kappa q^d  N^{2d} \le
562	((\cF^{\otimes q})^{\cdot d}).
563	\end{equation*}

565	bundle on $\overline{X_N}$ by a theorem of Siu \cite[Theorem
566	2.2.15]{PosAlgGeom}.  After possibly replacing $q$ by a multiple the line bundle
567	 $\cF^{\otimes q} \otimes \cM^{\otimes -qc_1N^2}$ admits a
568	non-zero global section. Say $h_{\overline{X_N},\cF}$ and
569	$h_{\overline{X_N},\cM}$ are representives of heights on
570	$\overline{X_N}$ attached by the Height Machine to

1	%% Section 5: N\'{e}ron--Tate height and height on the base
2	
3	
4	We keep the notation of $\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}. In
5	particular, $S$ is a regular, irreducible, quasi-projective variety over $\IQbar$ and
6	$\pi\colon\cA\rightarrow S$ is an abelian scheme of relative dimension
7	$g \ge 1$. Moreover, we have immersions as in
8	$\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq} and we
9	assume {\tt (Hyp)} from page~\pageref{def:hyp}.
10	We use the heights introduced in $\mathsection$\ref{SubsectionHeight}. 
11	Let $X$ be an irreducible closed subvariety of $\cA$ 
12	defined over $\IQbar$. We assume that $X$ dominates $S$ and is
13	non-degenerate as defined in Definition~\ref{DefinitionNonDegenerate}
14	
15	The upshot of {\tt (Hyp)} is that we obtain from Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm} the Betti form $\omega$ on $\an{\cA}$. Moreover, part (i) and (iii) of Proposition~\ref{PropBettiForm} implies that, for $d = \dim X$, 
16	\begin{equation}\label{EqBettiFormHtIneq}
17	\omega|_{X^{\mathrm{sm},\mathrm{an}}}^{\wedge d} > 0 \quad \text{ at some smooth point of }X^{\mathrm{an}}.
18	\end{equation}
19	
20	
21	Our assumption \eqref{EqBettiFormHtIneq} allows us to apply
22	Proposition~\ref{PropAuxHtIneq} to
23	$X$. 
24	 There exists a constant $c_1>0$ as in \eqref{EqConstantInHtIneq} such
25	 that the following holds. Let $N\in\IN$ be a power of $2$, there
26	 exists a Zariski open dense subset $U_N\subset X$ and a constant
27	 $c_2(N)\ge 0$ such that
28	\begin{equation}\label{EquationHeight1}
29	h([N]P) \ge c_1 N^2 h(\pi(P)) - c_2(N)
30	\end{equation}
31	for all $P\in U_N(\IQbar)$; we stress that $U_N$ and 
32	 $c_2\ge 0$ may depend  on $N$ in addition to $X,\cA,$ and the various
33	 immersions such as $\cA \subseteq \IP_\IQbar^n \times \IP_\IQbar^m$.
34	
35	
36	By the  Theorem of Silverman-Tate, see \cite[Theorem~A]{Silverman} and
37	Theorem~\ref{thm:silvermantate}, there exist a  constant
38	$c_0\ge 0$ %depending only on $\mathcal{A}/S$ and 
39	 such that
40	\begin{equation}
41	\label{EquationHeight2}
42	|\hat{h}_{\mathcal{A}}(P)-h(P)| \le
43	c_0\max\{1,h(\pi(P))\}\le c_0\bigl(1+h(\pi(P))\bigr)
44	% c_3 h_{\overline S,\mathcal{M}}(\pi(P))+c_4
45	\end{equation}
46	for all $P\in \mathcal{A}(\IQbar)$.
47	
48	
49	Next we kill Zimmer constants as in
50	Masser's~\cite[Appendix~C]{ZannierBook}.
51	For any $P\in U_N(\IQbar)$, we have
52	\begin{align*}
53	\hat{h}_{\cA}([N](P)) &\ge h([N](P))
54	-c_0\bigl(1+h(\pi([N](P)))\bigr) 
55	&&\text{(by (\ref{EquationHeight2}))}\\
56	&= h([N](P))
57	-c_0\bigl(1+h(\pi(P))\bigr)
58	&&\text{(as $\pi([N](P))=\pi(P)$)}
59	\\
60	&\ge c_1 N^2 h(\pi(P)) -c_2(N) -c_0\bigl(1+h(\pi(P))\bigr)
61	&&\text{(by (\ref{EquationHeight1})).}
62	\end{align*}
63	We use $\hat{h}_{\mathcal{A}}([N]P)=N^2 \hat{h}_{\mathcal{A}}(P)$, 
64	divide by $N^2$, and rearrange to get
65	\begin{equation*}%\label{EquationFinal}
66	\hat{h}_{\cA}(P) \ge \left(c_1 - \frac{c_0}{N^2} \right) h(\pi(P)) - \frac{c_2(N)+ c_0}{N^2}
67	\end{equation*}
68	for all $N\in\IN$ that are powers of $2$  and all $P \in U_N(\IQbar)$. 
69	
70	Recall that $c_0$ and $c_1$ are independent of $N$. We  fix $N\in\IN$ to be the least
71	power of $2$ such that $N^2\ge 2c_0/c_1$. As $h(\pi(P))$ is
72	non-negative we get  % that \eqref{EquationFinal} holds for $N'$, then
73	\[
74	\hat{h}_{\cA}(P) \ge \frac{c_1}{2} h(\pi(P)) - \frac{c_2(N)+ c_0}{N^2}
75	\]
76	for all
77	$P \in U_N(\IQbar)$. Since $N$ is now fixed, the Zariski open dense
78	subset $U_N$ of $X$ is also fixed.
79	The theorem follows after adjusting $c_1$ and $c_2$. 
80	\qed
81	%\end{proof}
82	\begin{remark}
83	In the proof of Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality} we can keep track of the
84	process to compute the constant $c_1 > 0$. Use the notation in
85	$\mathsection$\ref{SectionSettingUpHtIneq}. In particular $\omega$ is
86	the Betti form on $\cA$, we have an immersion $\cA \subseteq \IP_\IC^n
87	\times \IP_\IC^m$, $\alpha$ is a $(1,1)$-form on $\IP_\IC^n \times
88	\IP_\IC^m$ representing the Chern class of $\cO(1,1)$, $\Delta \subseteq S^{\mathrm{an}}$ is open and relative compact, and $\theta \colon \cA^{\mathrm{an}} \rightarrow [0,1]$ (which factors through $S^{\mathrm{an}}$) is a smooth function with compact support contained in $\cA_{\Delta} := \pi^{-1}(\Delta)$. The function $\theta$ should furthermore satisfy $\theta(P_0) = 1$ for some $P_0 \in X^{\mathrm{sm}}(\IC)$ such that $(\omega|_{X^{\mathrm{sm}}})^{\wedge d}$ is positive at $P_0$, where $d = \dim X$.
89	
90	Assume $d \ge 1$. The proof of Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality} tells us
91	that one half of any rational number satisfying the inequality
92	\eqref{EqConstantInHtIneq} can be taken as $c_1$. So the constant $c_1 > 0$ can be taken to be any rational
93	number
94	in  $(0,\kappa/(2c d))$, such that: %where  $\kappa$ and $c$ are:
95	\begin{itemize}
96	\item $\kappa = \kappa'/C^d$, where $\kappa' = \int_{\sman{X}}(
97	  \theta\omega )^{\wedge d}$, as in (\ref{eq:N2dgrowth}), and $C$ satisfies $C
98	  \alpha|_{\cA_{\Delta}} - \omega|_{\cA_{\Delta}} \ge 0$, as in (\ref{EqConditionOnCapitalC}),
99	\item $c$ is a  constant depending on a certain degree of  $X$ and coming from
100	  (\ref{eq:intersectionnumberub2}). % $c' = D^{\prime d-1}\binom{n+d}{n} (\cO(1,1)^{\cdot d}[\overline{X}])$, where $D'$ is such that the bihomogeneous polynomials defining $[2] \colon \cA \rightarrow \cA$ have bi-degree $(D',4)$.
101	\end{itemize}
102	\end{remark}

3	\label{subsec:univfamily}
4	In this section, we fix the basic setup to prove 
5	Proposition~\ref{PropAlgPtFar}, described as the alternative on page~\pageref{eq:alternative}, and
6	our main results.

8	Fix an integer $g \ge 2$.
9	Recall from $\mathsection$\ref{subsec:hgtineqnondeg} that
10	$\mathbb{M}_g$ 
11	denotes the fine moduli space of smooth curves of genus $g$,
12	with level-$\ell$-structure where $\ell\ge \bestlevel$ is fixed, \textit{cf.}
13	 \cite[Chapter~XVI, Theorem~2.11 (or above
14	Proposition~2.8)]{ACG:Curve},
15	 \cite[(5.14)]{DM:irreducibility}, or \cite[Theorem
16	 1.8]{OortSteenbrink}. It is known that 
17	$\mathbb{M}_g$ is a regular, quasi-projective variety of dimension
18	$3g-3$. We regard it over $\IQbar$; it is irreducible according
19	to our convention introduced below Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality}. 
20	%It is irreducible by \cite[Theorem 5.15]{DM:irreducibility}.
21	There exists a universal curve $\mathfrak{C}_g$ over
22	$\mathbb{M}_g$,  it is smooth and proper over
23	$\mathbb{M}_g$ and its fibers are smooth curves of genus
24	$g$. Moreover, $\mathfrak{C}_g\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ is
25	projective, \textit{cf.} \cite[Corollary to Theorem
26	1.2]{DM:irreducibility} or  \cite[Remark 2, \S 9.3]{NeronModels}.
27	
28	Denote by $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g)$ the relative Jacobian of
29	$\mathfrak{C}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$. It is an abelian scheme
30	coming with a natural principal polarization and equipped with level-$\ell$-structure, see
31	%see \cite[Proposition 4, \S 9.4]{NeronModels} or
32	\cite[Proposition 6.9]{MFK:GIT94}.

Since only finitely many pieces, OK.}}  Then $\epsilon$ gives rise to an Abel-Jacobi embedding $\mathfrak{C}_g \rightarrow \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g)$, which is fiberwise the classical Abel-Jacobi embedding. Note that $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g) \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ is the a principally polarized abelian scheme. From now on by abuse of notation, we denote by $\mathfrak{C}_g$ its image under the Abel-Jacobi embedding. 177	  \hat h \colon \mathfrak{A}_{g}(\IQbar)\rightarrow [0,\infty).
178	\end{equation}
179	
180	%% Instead of twisting by a power of $\pi^*(\cM|_{\mathbb{A}_g})$ we
181	%% could have instead embedded $\mathfrak{A}_g\rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$
182	%% over members of a finite open affine cover of $\mathbb{A}_g$. This
183	%% would leads to the same global N\'eron--Tate height 
184	%% as $\pi^*(\cM|_{\mathbb{A}_g})$ restricted to a single
185	%% $\mathfrak{A}_{g,s}$ is trivial. 
186	
187	
188	
189	Let $M\ge 1$ be an integer. We write $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}$ for the
190	$M$-fold fibered power
191	$\mathfrak{A}_g\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} \cdots \times_{\mathbb{A}_g}\mathfrak{A}_g$
192	over $\mathbb{A}_g$.
193	Then $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$ is an abelian
194	scheme.
195	% Similarly, we define $\mathfrak{C}_g^{[M]}$ to be the $M$-fold fibered
196	% power $\mathfrak{C}_g\times_{\mathbb{M}_g}\cdots
197	% \times_{\mathbb{M}_g}\mathfrak{C}_g$.
198	
199	
200	By taking the product we obtain closed
201	immersions $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\rightarrow (\IP_\IQbar^n)^{M}\times
202	\mathbb{A}_g$. The fiber of $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\rightarrow
203	\mathbb{A}_g$ above $s\in \mathbb{A}_g(\IQbar)$ is the $M$-fold power of
204	$\mathfrak{A}_{g,s}$. The associated fiberwise N\'eron--Tate height
205	$\hat h \colon \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}(\IQbar)\rightarrow [0,\infty)$ is
206	the sum of the N\'eron--Tate heights, as in (\ref{eq:NTfinemoduli}),
207	of the $M$ coordinates.%\Pcomment{Replaced $m$ by $M$ twice (typo).}
208	
209	Let us now define the Faltings--Zhang morphism. 
210	In our setting the relative Picard scheme
211	$\mathrm{Pic}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$ exists as a group scheme
212	over $\mathbb{M}_g$. It is a union over all $p\in\IZ$
213	of open and closed subschemes
214	$\mathrm{Pic}^p(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$, where $p$ indicates the
215	degree of a line bundle.
216	By definition we have
217	$\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g) =
218	\mathrm{Pic}^0(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$.
219	We cannot expect to
220	have a section of $\mathfrak{C}_g\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g$,
221	so we cannot expect to find an immersion of $\mathfrak{C}_g$
222	into $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$. 
223	As constructed in the proof of
224	\cite[Proposition~6.9]{MFK:GIT94} we
225	do have a morphism $\mathfrak{C}_g \rightarrow
226	\mathrm{Pic}^1(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$ over $\mathbb{M}_g$.
227	Let $\mathfrak{C}_g^{[M]}$ and
228	$\mathrm{Pic}^p(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)^{[M]}$ denote the
229	respective $M$-th fibered powers over $\mathbb{M}_g$. 
230	The difference morphism coming from the group scheme law
231	$\mathrm{Pic}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)\times_{\mathbb{M}_g}
232	\mathrm{Pic}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)\rightarrow
233	\mathrm{Pic}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$ restricts to a morphism
234	$\mathrm{Pic}^1(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)\times_{\mathbb{M}_g}
235	\mathrm{Pic}^1(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)
236	\rightarrow
237	\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$ of schemes
238	over $\mathbb{M}_g$.
239	We take the appropriate  product morphism over $\mathbb{M}_g$ to get a
240	morphism 
241	\begin{equation}
242	  \label{eq:prefaltingszhang}
243	  \mathfrak{C}_g^{[M+1]} \rightarrow
244	 \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)^{[M]}
245	\end{equation}
246	over $\mathbb{M}_g$. The choice of product is modeled after
247	(\ref{eq:faltingszhangintro}). More precisely, consider the situation
248	above a $k$-point of $\mathbb{M}_g$, where $k$ is an algebraically
249	closed field. The fiber of $\mathfrak{C}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ above this point is a smooth curve $C$
250	defined over $k$ of genus $g$. For $P_0,\ldots,P_M\in C(k)$ the
251	morphism (\ref{eq:prefaltingszhang}) maps $(P_0,P_1,\ldots,P_M)
252	\mapsto (P_1-P_0,P_2-P_0,\ldots,P_M-P_0)$ where the difference takes
253	place in the Jacobian of $C$. %\Gcomment{Slightly rephrased this.}
254	
255	Recall (\ref{EqUnivJac}). We take the $M$-fold product and compose
256	with (\ref{eq:prefaltingszhang}) to obtain a commutative diagram of
257	morphisms of schemes %\Ginlinecomment{Removed the notation $\mathscr{D}_M$ in the diagram.}
258	\begin{equation}
259	  \label{DefFaltingsZhang}
260	  \begin{aligned}
261	    \xymatrix{
262	      \mathfrak{C}_g^{[M+1]}\ar[r] \ar[d] &
263	      \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}
264	      \ar[d] \\  
265	      \mathbb{M}_g \ar[r]_{\tau} & \mathbb{A}_g.
266	    }
267	  \end{aligned}
268	\end{equation}
269	
270	If $S\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ is a morphism of schemes then we define
271	$\mathfrak{C}_S = \mathfrak{C}_g \times_{\mathbb{M}_g} S$ and
272	$\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M]} = \mathfrak{C}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{M}_g} S$.
273	If $S$ is irreducible, then so is $\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M]}$ by induction
274	on $M$ and a topological argument using that
275	$\mathfrak{C}_S\rightarrow S$ is smooth and hence open.
276	Taking the fibered product with $S$  and composing with (\ref{DefFaltingsZhang})
277	yields a commutative diagram of morphisms of schemes
278	\begin{equation*}
279	%  \label{DefFaltingsZhang2}
280	  \begin{aligned}
281	    \xymatrix{
282	      \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}\ar[r] \ar[d] &
283	      \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}
284	      \ar[d] \\  
285	      S\ar[r]_{\tau\circ(S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g)} & \mathbb{A}_g.
286	    }
287	  \end{aligned}
288	\end{equation*}
289	% we call it Faltings--Zhang morphism and also denote it by
290	% $\mathscr{D}_M$.
291	% \Pcomment{Here's a second variant of Faltings--Zhang defined on a
292	%   different source.}
293	% There is no reason to expect $\mathscr{D}_M$ to be proper. Here is an
294	% alternative construction that is proper.
295	% We continue with
296	% (\ref{DefFaltingsZhang2}) and use the
297	By the
298	universal property
299	of the fibered product we get a morphism of schemes 
300	\begin{equation}
301	  \label{DefFaltingsZhang3}
302	  \begin{aligned}
303	    \xymatrix{
304	      \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}\ar[r]^-{\mathscr{D}_{M}}
305	\ar[dr]  &
306	      \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}      \times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S
307	      \ar[d] \\  
308	      & S
309	    }
310	  \end{aligned}
311	\end{equation}
312	over $S$. We call $\mathscr{D}_{M}$ the \textit{Faltings--Zhang morphism} (over
313	$S$).
314	Then $\mathscr{D}_{M}$  is proper since the diagonal arrow in
315	(\ref{DefFaltingsZhang3}) is proper.
316	
317	% Finally, let us consider
318	% the morphism
319	% $$\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M+1]} \rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}$$
320	% defined fiberwise over $\mathbb{A}_g$ by
321	% \begin{equation}
322	% \label{eq:faltingszhang0}
323	% (P_0,P_1,\ldots,P_M) \mapsto (P_1-P_0,\ldots,P_M-P_0).
324	% \end{equation}
325	%  %This morphism is over $\IA_g$ and it is proper. 
326	% In fact, we will need it as a morphism on $\mathfrak{C}_g^{[M+1]}$. 
327	% Note that  there is no canonical embedding
328	% of $\mathfrak{C}_g$ in $\mathfrak{A}_g$ as we may have no section to
329	% construct the Abel--Jacobi map. 
330	% However,
331	% we do have a morphism
332	% $\mathfrak{C}_g \rightarrow
333	% \mathrm{Pic}^1(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$ to the line bundles of
334	% degree $1$;
335	% see the proof of \cite[Proposition~6.9]{MFK:GIT94}. 
336	% The difference of two sections of
337	% $\mathrm{Pic}^1(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$
338	% lies in $\mathrm{Pic}^0(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g) = \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g)$.
339	% For each integer $M\ge 2$  the map
340	% (\ref{eq:faltingszhang0}) yields a well-defined morphism
341	% \begin{equation}
342	% \label{DefFaltingsZhang}
343	% \mathscr{D}_M\colon \mathfrak{C}_g^{[M+1]}\rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}
344	% \end{equation}
345	% called the  \textit{$M$-th Faltings--Zhang} morphism.
346	% By abuse of notation we also write $\mathscr{D}_M$ for the 
347	% morphism $\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}\rightarrow \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}
348	% \times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S$
349	% induced by the base change by
350	% $S\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g\xrightarrow{\tau} \mathbb{A}_g$.
351	
352	Let for the moment $S\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ be the identity.
353	If $s \in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$, then $\mathfrak{C}_s$ is the curve
354	parametrized by $s$, and $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$ is its Jacobian.
355	To embed $\mathfrak{C}_s$ into $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$ we must work with a
356	base point $P\in
357	\mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$. Then 
358	$\mathfrak{C}_s-P = \mathscr{D}_1(\{P\} \times \mathfrak{C}_s)$
359	is an irreducible curve inside $\mathfrak{A}_g$ lying above $\tau(s)$.
360	Hence it provides a closed immersion   $\mathfrak{C}_s-P \subset
361	\IP_\IQbar^n$.%\Pcomment{We have to adapt this paragraph if we work
362	%  with the second FZ morphism.}\Gcomment{I think that this paragraph is OK because $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]} \times_{\mathbb{A}_g}\{s\}$ is just one abelian variety, and hence equals the fiber $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}^{[M]}$.}
363	
364	%Say $i\in \{1,\ldots,t\}$ such that $\tau(s) \in V_i(\IQbar)$. Then the construction above provides a closed immersion   $\mathfrak{C}_s-P \subset \IP_\IQbar^n$.
365	% If we are given $i$ such that $s\in S_i(\IQbar)$, then
366	% we may consider $\mathfrak{C}_s-Q$ as an irreducible curve in $\IP^n_\IQbar$.
367	
368	Let $\deg X$ denote the degree of an irreducible closed subvariety $X$ of
369	$\IP^n_\IQbar$ and let $h(X)$ denote its height, \textit{cf.} \cite{BGS}.
370	
371	%\Pinlinecomment{TODO: Get degree bound for $P_s$ replaced by any $P$.}
372	\begin{lemma}
373	  \label{lem:uniformdegreebound}
374	  There exists a constant $c$ such that the following two properties
375	  hold for all $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$.
376	  \begin{enumerate}
377	  \item [(i)] We have
378	    $\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P) \le c$
379	    for all $P\in
380	    \mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}(\IQbar)$.
381	  \item[(ii)]
382	    There exists $P_s\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$ such that
383	    $h(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s) \le  c \max\{1,
384	    h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}$.    
385	  \end{enumerate}
386	  % such that if  $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$, then 
387	  % there exists $P_s\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$
388	  % such that\Pcomment{TODO: we should now argument with $s\in
389	  %   S(\IQbar)$ and not $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$.} \Gcomment{Maybe we only need to work with $s \in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$ as from Section 7 $S$ is always contained in $\mathbb{M}_g$. However to ``prove'' Thm 6.2, we need to first of all take a finite covering $S'$ of $S$, and thus it's better to state Thm 6.2 under the setup $S\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$.}
390	  % \begin{equation*}
391	  %   \deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)\le c\quad\text{and}\quad 
392	  %   h(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s) \le  c \max\{1,
393	  %   h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}.
394	  % \end{equation*}
395	\end{lemma}
396	\begin{proof}
397	%  !!!TODO: make sure this lemma now works in the new notation.
398	  We need a quasi-section of
399	  $\mathfrak{C}_g\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g$ as provided by \cite[Corollaire~17.16.3(ii)]{EGAIV}. 
400	  So there is an affine scheme $S$
401	  and a morphism $S\rightarrow \mathfrak{C}_g$ that factors through a
402	  surjective, quasi-finite, \'etale morphism $ S\rightarrow
403	  \mathbb{M}_g$. We consider the product
404	  $\mathfrak{C}_g\times_{\mathbb{M}_g} S\rightarrow
405	  \mathfrak{C}_g^{[2]}$ composed with the Faltings--Zhang morphism
406	  $\mathscr{D}_1\colon
407	  \mathfrak{C}_g^{[2]}\rightarrow\mathfrak{A}_g\times_{\IA_g}
408	  \mathbb{M}_g$ over $\mathbb{M}_g$ and then the projection of
409	  $\mathfrak{A}_g$. This is a morphism of schemes
410	  $\mathfrak{C}_g\times_{\mathbb{M}_g} S\rightarrow
411	  \mathfrak{A}_g$. Its 
412	  image is a constructible subset of $\mathfrak{A}_g$. 
413	  So it is a union of finitely many 
414	  irreducible Zariski locally closed subsets $\{X_i\}_i$ of
415	  $\mathfrak{A}_g$. We have
416	  the following property. %\Pinlinecomment{I slightly changed the
417	 %   argument here.}
418	
419	  Given a point $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$, there is an $i$ such that
420	  the fiber of $\pi|_{X_i}\colon X_i \rightarrow\mathbb{A}_g$ above
421	  $\tau(s)$ is a finite union of irreducible curves, up-to finitely
422	  many points one of these curves is
423	  $\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s$ with $P_s\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$.
424	  
425	  
426	  We  have a closed immersion $\mathfrak{A}_g\rightarrow \IP_\IQbar^n
427	  \times \mathbb{A}_g$.
428	  Moreover, a sufficiently  large positive power of $\cM$ induces a
429	  closed immersion of $\overline{\mathbb{A}_g}\rightarrow\IP_\IQbar^m$
430	   for some $m$.
431	  Thus, we consider $\mathbb{A}_g$ as a 
432	  Zariski locally closed subset
433	  $\IP_\IQbar^m$. We identify each 
434	  $X_i$ with its image in $\IP^n_\IQbar\times
435	  \IP^m_\IQbar$, an irreducible  Zariski locally closed set.  Then
436	  $\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s \subset\IP^n_\IQbar$ arises as an irreducible
437	  component of the intersection of some Zariski
438	  closure $\overline{X_i}$
439	   with $\IP^n_\IQbar\times
440	  \{\tau(s)\}$.
441	
442	  We use the Segre embedding
443	  $\IP_\IQbar^n\times\IP_\IQbar^m\rightarrow \IP_\IQbar^{(n+1)(m+1)-1}$
444	  to embed our situation into projective space. By B\'ezout's Theorem
445	  \cite[Example~8.4.6]{Fulton},
446	  $\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)$ is bounded from above uniformly in $s$.
447	  Translating a curve inside $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$ by a point of
448	  $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}(\IQbar)$ does not
449	  change its degree. So if $P\in \mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$, then
450	  $\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P)=\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)$.  
451	  This yields (i).
452	
453	  Part (ii) follows as (i) but this time we use
454	  the Arithmetic B\'ezout Theorem, still executing the intersection after
455	  applying the Segre embedding. Indeed, recall that
456	  $\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s$ as an irreducible
457	  of the intersection of some $\overline{X_i}$ with
458	  $\IP_\IQbar^n\times\{\tau(s)\}$. 
459	  The height and degree of $\overline{X_i}$ are
460	  bounded from above independently of $s$; the same holds for the
461	  degree of $\IP_\IQbar^n \times\{\tau(s)\}$. The height of
462	  $\IP_\IQbar^n \times\{\tau(s)\}$ is bounded from above linearly in terms of
463	  $h(\tau(s))$. Finally, we can apply \cite[Th\'eor\`eme
464	  3]{HauteursAlt3}.
465	  Finally, note that  by the Height
466	  Machine the absolute logarithmic Weil height $h( \tau(s))$,
467	  where $\tau(s)$ is understood as an element of
468	  $\IP^m_{\IQbar}(\IQbar)$, is bounded from
469	  above linearly in terms of $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))$. 
470	\end{proof}
471	
472	\subsection{Non-degeneracy of $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$
473	  for large $M$}  
474	  In this subsection all varieties  are defined over the field $\IC$. 
475	We keep the notation of the previous subsection and let
476	$S$ be an irreducible variety with a quasi-finite %\Pcomment{Changed
477	%  ``finite'' to ``quasi-finite''. Is this OK?}
478	   morphism
479	$S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g$. %In this subsection, as in \cite{GaoBettiRank} we take $\IC$ as the base field, \textit{i.e.}, all varieties and morphisms are (viewed as) defined over $\IC$. 
480	Note that
481	$\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ is Zariski closed
482	in $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S$ 
483	because $\mathscr{D}_M$ is proper. We endow this image with the
484	reduced induced scheme structure.
485	
486	The following non-degeneracy theorem proved by the second-named author
487	is crucial to prove our main result.
488	It confirms that
489	Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality} can be applied to
490	$\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ for $M \ge 3g-2$.
491	We obtain a height inequality on a Zariski open dense subset. 
492	%\Pcomment{I added the comment on the reduced induced structure, as
493	%  proposed by referee .4. FZ is proper as we're using the second
494	%  definition.}
495	
496	\begin{theorem}[\!\!{\cite[Theorem~1.2']{GaoBettiRank}}]\label{ThmNonDegForBd}
497	Let $S$ be an irreducible variety with a (not necessarily dominant) quasi-finite morphism $S \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$. Assume $g \ge 2$ and $M
498	\ge 3g-2$. Then $\mathscr{D}_M( \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]} )$, which is a closed 
499	irreducible subvariety of $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S$, is 
500	non-degenerate in the sense of
501	Definition~\ref{DefinitionNonDegenerate}.
502	\end{theorem}
503	
504	The fibered product in the theorem involves
505	$S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g\xrightarrow{\tau} \mathbb{A}_g$.
506	
507	More precisely, the meaning of the conclusion of the theorem is as follows. For the abelian scheme $\pi \colon \cA = \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]} \times_{\mathbb{A}_g}S \rightarrow S$ and for the irreducible subvariety $X:=\mathscr{D}_M( \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]} )$ of $\cA$, there exists a open non-empty subset $\Delta$ of $\an{S}$, with the Betti map $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} = \pi^{-1}(\Delta) \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$, such that 
508	\begin{equation}\label{EqNonDegXDMC}
509	\mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{\sman{X}})_x = 2\dim X\quad \text{for some }x \in \sman{X} \cap \cA_{\Delta},
510	\end{equation}
511	when $g \ge 2$ and $M \ge 3g-2$.
512	
513	
514	\begin{proof}
515	This theorem, essentially \cite[Theorem~1.2']{GaoBettiRank}, is a consequence of Theorem~1.3 of \textit{loc.cit.} Because of its importance to the current paper, we hereby give more details of the deduction.
516	
517	We start by showing the result for the case where $\mathfrak{C}_S
518	\rightarrow S$ admits a section $\epsilon$. %\Pcomment{I think we show  something stronger here, namely that 6.2 holds under the weake  assumption that $S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g$ is quasi-finite (but with  the additional assumption on the section). Is this correct? And, if yes, does Thm 6.2 hold with $S\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ quasi-finite?, If so, we should state this.}
519	In this case $\epsilon$ induces an Abel--Jacobi embedding $j_{\epsilon} \colon \mathfrak{C}_S \rightarrow \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S)$, which is a closed immersion of $S$-schemes. The modular map is the Cartesian diagram
520	\[
521	\xymatrix{
522	\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S) \ar[r]^-{\iota} \ar[d] \pullbackcorner & \mathfrak{A}_g \ar[d] \\
523	S \ar[r] & \mathbb{A}_g
524	}
525	\]
526	with the bottom morphism being the composite of the given $S \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ with the Torelli map $\tau \colon \mathbb{M}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$. The Torelli map $\tau$ is quasi-finite; see \cite[Lemma 1.11]{OortSteenbrink}. Thus the bottom morphism is quasi-finite. Hence $\iota$ is quasi-finite.
527	
528	We wish to apply \cite[Theorem~1.3]{GaoBettiRank} to the subvariety $j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)$ of the abelian scheme $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S) \rightarrow S$. We need to verify the hypotheses. First of all $\iota|_{j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)}$ is generically finite because $\iota$ is quasi-finite. Hypothesis (a) is satisfied since $\dim j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S) = \dim S + 1 > \dim S$. For hypotheses (b) and (c), note that for any $s\in S(\IC)$, the fiber $j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)_s$ is the Abel--Jacobi embedding of $\mathfrak{C}_s$ in its Jacobian via the point $\epsilon(s)$. Thus hypothesis (b) is satisfied because each curve generates its Jacobian, and hypothesis (c) holds true since $g \ge 2$.
529	
530	Thus we can apply \cite[Theorem~1.3.(ii)]{GaoBettiRank} and obtain that $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ is non-degenerate\footnote{Observe that $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}) = \mathscr{D}_M^{\cA}(j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}))$, with $\mathscr{D}_M^{\cA}$ be as in \cite[Theorem~1.3.(ii)]{GaoBettiRank} with $\cA = \mathfrak{A}_g\times_{\mathbb{A}_g}S \cong \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S)$. See below \eqref{eq:prefaltingszhang}.} if $M \ge j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S) = \dim S + 1$. But $\dim S \le \dim \mathbb{M}_g = 3g-3$. Hence  $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ is non-degenerate if $M \ge 3g-2$.
531	
532	%As $M \ge 3g-2 = \dim \mathbb{M}_g +1 \ge \dim S+1$, \cite[Theorem~1.2']{GaoBettiRank} implies that the theorem holds true if $\mathfrak{C}_S \rightarrow S$ admits a section.
533	
534	For an arbitrary $S$, the generic fiber of $\mathfrak{C}_S \rightarrow
535	S$ has a rational point over some finite extension of $K(S)$, the
536	function field of $S$. Thus there exists a quasi-finite \'{e}tale
537	dominant (not necessarily surjective) morphism $\rho \colon S'
538	\rightarrow S$, with $S'$ irreducible, such that $\mathfrak{C}_{S'} = \mathfrak{C}_S \times_S
539	S' \rightarrow S'$ admits a section. Thus $X' :=
540	\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_{S'}^{[M+1]})$, as a subvariety of
541	$\cA':=\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S'$,  is
542	non-degenerate by the previous case. So there exists a connected, open non-empty subset $\Delta'$ of $\an{S'}$, with the Betti map $b_{\Delta'} \colon \cA'_{\Delta'} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$, such that for some $x' \in \sman{X'} \cap \cA'_{\Delta'}$ we have
543	\[
544	\mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta'}|_{\sman{X'}})_{x'} = 2\dim X'.
545	\]
546	We may furthermore shrink $\Delta'$ so that $\rho|_{\Delta'}$ is a diffeomorphism. In particular $\Delta:=\rho(\Delta')$ is open in $\an{S}$.
547	
548	Denote by $\rho'_{\cA} \colon \cA' = \cA\times_S S' \rightarrow \cA$
549	the projection to the first factor. Then
550	$\rho'_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}}$ is a diffeomorphism. Both $\cA
551	\rightarrow S$ and $\cA' \rightarrow S'$ carry
552	level-$\ell$-structures. By construction and uniqueness properties of
553	the Betti map, we may assume that $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ equals $b_{\Delta'} \circ  (\rho'_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}})^{-1}$. Thus
554	\[
555	\mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{\sman{X}})_{x} = 2\dim X'
556	\]
557	with $x = \rho_{\cA}(x')$. So \eqref{EqNonDegXDMC} holds true because $\dim X' = \dim X$. Hence we are done.
558	\end{proof}

562	\subsection{Technical lemmas}
563	
564	
565	The following lemma will be useful in the proofs of the desired
566	bounds. Let for the moment $k$ be an algebraically closed field and
567	$M\ge 1,n\ge 1$ integers. If $Z$ is a Zariski closed subset of $(\IP_k^n)^M$
568	we let $\deg Z$ denote the sum of the degrees of all irreducible
569	components of $Z$ with respect to $\cO(1,\ldots,1)$. 
570	
571	%\marginpar{\tiny{Noga Alon. Should be a rather elementary result.}}
572	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaNAlon} 
573	  Let $C \subset \IP_k^n$ be an
574	  irreducible curve defined over $k$ and let $Z \subseteq (\IP_k^n)^M$
575	  be a Zariski closed subset of $(\IP_k^n)^M$ such that $C^M = C \times
576	  \cdots \times C \not\subseteq Z$. Then there exists a number $B$,
577	  depending only on $M, \deg C,$ and $\deg Z$, satisfying the following
578	  property. If $\Sigma \subset C(k)$ has cardinality $\ge B$, then
579	  $\Sigma^M = \Sigma \times \cdots \times \Sigma \not\subseteq Z(k)$.
580	\end{lemma}
581	\begin{proof}%\Pcomment{I reworded part of this  proof, we don't need flatness or that $C$ is smooth.}
582	  Let us prove this lemma by induction on $M$. The case $M = 1$ follows
583	  easily from B\'{e}zout's Theorem.
584	
585	  Assume the lemma is proved for $1, \ldots, M-1$. Let $q \colon
586	  (\IP_k^n)^M \rightarrow \IP_k^n$ be the projection to the first
587	  factor.
588	
589	  The number of irreducible components of $Z \cap C^M$ and their degrees
590	  are bounded from above in terms of $M,\deg C,$ and $\deg Z$ by
591	  B\'ezout's Theorem applied to the Segre embedding.
592	  Let $Z'$ be the union of all  irreducible
593	  components $Y$ of $Z \cap C^M$ with $\dim q(Y)\ge 1$, let $Z''$ be the
594	  union of all other irreducible components.
595	  % Observe that $q(Y)\subset C$ and $\dim Y \le M-1$ by hypothesis.
596	
597	  Note that $q(Z')\subset C$. For all $P \in C(k)$ the fiber
598	  $q|_{Z'}^{-1}(P)=Z'\cap (\{P\}\times (\IP_k^n)^{M-1})$ has
599	  dimension at most
600	  $\dim Z'-1\le M-2$. So the projection of $q|_{Z'}^{-1}(P)$ to the final
601	  factors $(\IP_k^n)^{M-1}$ does not contain $C^{M-1}$. By B\'ezout's
602	  Theorem the degree of this projection is bounded in terms of $M,\deg
603	  C,$ and $\deg Z$. We apply the induction hypothesis to the projection
604	  of $q|_Y^{-1}(P)$ to $(\IP_k^n)^{M-1}$ and obtain a number $B'$,
605	  depending only on $M,\deg C,$ and $\deg Z$ satisfying the following
606	  property. If $\Sigma \subseteq C(k)$ has cardinality $\ge B'$, then
607	  $\{P\} \times \Sigma^{M-1} \not\subseteq Z'(k)$ for all $P \in C(k)$.
608	  % In particular, $\Sigma^M\not \subset Y(k)$, which concludes this case.
609	
610	  Now $\dim q(Z'') = 0$, so $q(Z'')$ is a finite set of cardinality
611	  at most $B''$, the number of irreducible components of
612	  $Z\cap C^M$. 
613	
614	  The lemma follows with $B=\max\{B',B''+1\}$.  
615	\end{proof}
616	
617	In the next lemma we use the Faltings--Zhang morphism in a single
618	abelian variety $A$, \textit{i.e.}, $\mathscr{D}_M \colon
619	A^{M+1}\rightarrow A^M$ defined by $(P_0,\ldots,P_M)\mapsto
620	(P_1-P_0,\ldots,P_M-P_0)$. 
621	
622	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaPacketsAlternative3}
623	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety defined over $\overline{\IQ}$ and
624	  suppose $C$ is a smooth curve of genus $g\ge
625	  2$ contained in $A$. If $Z$ is an irreducible Zariski
626	  closed and proper subset of $\mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})$, 
627	  then
628	%in $A^M$, then
629	  \begin{equation*}
630	    \#\{ P \in C(\overline{\IQ}) : (C-P)^M \subset Z
631	%    \subsetneq \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})
632	    \} \le 84(g-1).
633	  \end{equation*}
634	\end{lemma}
635	\begin{proof}
636	For simplicity denote by $\Xi = \{ P \in C(\overline{\IQ}) : (C-P)^M
637	\subset Z \}$. % \subsetneq \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1}) \}$.
638	%
639	Fix $P_0\in \Xi$. It suffices to prove that there are only $84(g-1)$
640	possibilities for $P_1 - P_0$ when $P_1$ runs over $\Xi$.
641	
642	Say $P_1 \in \Xi$ and let $i\in \{0,1\}$. 
643	Note that
644	$Z\subsetneq \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})$ and so
645	 $\dim Z < \dim \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})\le M+1$, 
646	as $\mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})$ irreducible. 
647	Note that $(C-P_i)^M\subset Z$, and so both have dimension $M$.
648	As $Z$ is irreducible we find 
649	 $(C - P_i)^M=Z$ for $i=0$ and $i=1$.
650	
651	Applying the first projection  $A^M\rightarrow A$ yields $C-P_1=C-P_0$. In other words, $P_1-P_0$
652	stabilizes $C$. By Hurwitz's Theorem \cite{Hurwitz1892}, a smooth curve over $\IQbar$ of
653	genus $g\ge 2$ has at most $84(g-1)$ automorphisms.
654	Hence we are done.
655	\end{proof}

1	%% Section 7: N\'{e}ron-Tate distance between points on curves
2	
3	The goal of this section is to prove Proposition~\ref{PropAlgPtFar}, below.
4	Namely we will show that $\IQbar$-points on smooth curves are
5	rather ``sparse'', in the sense that the N\'{e}ron--Tate distance
6	between two $\IQbar$-points on a smooth curve $C$ is in
7	general large compared with the Weil height of $C$. %% Now we
8	%% shall prove the following equivalently form of
9	%% Proposition~\ref{PropNTDistance}.
10	%\Gcomment{I think it's better not to assume $S$ to be open, as we use induction on $\dim S$ to prove the proposition. Maybe good to relax the assumption on $S$ in Section 3.}
11	
12	
13	We use the notation from $\mathsection$\ref{subsec:univfamily}. Recall
14	that we have fixed a projective compactification
15	$\overline{\mathbb{A}_g}$ of $\mathbb{A}_g$ over $\IQbar$, an ample
16	line bundle $\cM$, and a height function
17	$h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM} \colon
18	\mathbb{A}_g(\IQbar)\rightarrow \IR$ attached to this pair.
19	We also fixed a closed immersion $\mathfrak{A}_g$ into
20	$\IP^n_\IQbar\times \mathbb{A}_g$ over $\mathbb{A}_g$ and
21	let $\tau\colon \mathbb{M}_g\rightarrow\mathbb{A}_g$ denote the
22	Torelli morphism. 
23	If $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$ then $\mathfrak{C}_s$
24	is a smooth curve of genus $g$ defined over $\IQbar$.
25	Moreover, if $P,Q\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$, then $P-Q$ is a
26	well-defined element of $\mathfrak{A}_g(\IQbar)$ and so is its
27	N\'eron--Tate height  $\hat h(P-Q)$, see (\ref{eq:NTfinemoduli}).
28	
29	\begin{proposition}\label{PropAlgPtFar}
30	  Let $S$ be an irreducible closed subvariety of $\mathbb{M}_g$ defined over $\IQbar$. There
31	  exist positive constants $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$ depending on the choices
32	  made above and on $S$ with the following property. Let $s\in
33	  S(\IQbar)$ with $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))
34	  \ge c_1$. There exists a subset $\Xi_s \subset \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$
35	  with $\#\Xi_s\le c_2$ such that any $P \in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$
36	  satisfies the following alternative.
37	  \begin{enumerate}
38	  \item[(i)] Either $P \in \Xi_s$;
39	  \item[(ii)] or $\#\{Q \in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar) : \hat{h}(Q-P) \le h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))/c_3\} < c_4$.
40	  \end{enumerate}
41	\end{proposition}
42

55	%% inequality follows from basic height properties that are essentially

57

61	\begin{proof}
62	  We fix an immersion of $\mathbb{M}_g$ into
63	  some projective space and let $\overline{\mathbb{M}_g}$ denote its
64	  Zariski closure.

66	% and an ample line bundle $\mathcal{N}$ on $\overline{\mathbb{M}_g}$.
67	% The Height Machine provides a height function
68	% $h_{\overline{\mathbb{M}_g},\mathcal{N}} \colon
69	% \overline{\mathbb{M}_g}(\IQbar) \rightarrow \IR$.  
70	By a standard triangle inequality estimate, there exist constants
71	$c''>0$ and $c'''\ge 0$ such that

75	for all $s \in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$; see

81	then $h(s) \ge c''

104	%$\cA= \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S^{\mathrm{sm}}$

108	$X$

111	$c > 0$ and $c'$ as well as a Zariski open dense subset $U$ of
112	%$X=\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_{S^{\mathrm{sm}}}^{[M+1]})$,
113	$X$,
114	satisfying the following property.
115	%For all $s \in S^{\mathrm{sm}}(\IQbar)$ and
116	For all $s \in S(\IQbar)$ and
117	all $P, Q_1,\ldots,Q_M \in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$, we have
118	\begin{equation}\label{EqHtInequalityBdRatPt} 
119	%c h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s)) \le
120	c h(s)\le 
121	\hat{h}(Q_1-P) + \cdots + \hat{h}(Q_M - P) + c' \quad
122	\text{if}\quad (Q_1-P,\ldots, Q_M-P) \in U(\IQbar).
123	\end{equation}
124	
125	Observe that $\pi(X)=S^{\mathrm{sm}}$, where
126	%Observe that $\pi(X)=S$, where
127	%$\pi\colon \mathfrak{A}_{g}^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S\rightarrow
128	%S$
129	$\pi\colon \mathcal{A} \rightarrow S^{\mathrm{sm}}$ 
130	is the structure
131	morphism.
132	Therefore, $S \setminus \pi(U)$ is not Zariski dense in
133	$S$. %\Pcomment{I don't see why $\pi(U)$ is open, but it doesn't matter.} 
134	Let $S_1,\ldots, S_r$ be the irreducible components of the
135	Zariski closure of $S\setminus \pi(U)$ in $S$. Then $\dim S_j \le \dim
136	S - 1$ for all $j$.
137	
138	By the induction hypothesis, this proposition holds for all $S_j$. Thus it
139	remains to prove the conclusion of this proposition for curves above
140	\begin{equation}
141	\label{eq:soutsideSi}

143	\end{equation}
144	 First we construct $\Xi_s$ and then
145	we will show that we are in one of the two alternatives. 
146	
147	It is convenient to fix a base point $P_s\in
148	\mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$ and consider $(\mathfrak{C}_s - P_s)^M$
149	as a subvariety of $\cA_s = \pi^{-1}(s)$. 
150	% as a curve
151	%inside $(\mathfrak{A}_g)_{\tau(s)}$ via the Abel--Jacobi map.
152	
153	Let us set $W = X\setminus U$, it is a Zariski closed and proper
154	subset of $X$.  By (\ref{eq:soutsideSi}) we find  $W_s\subsetneq X_s = \mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_s^{[M+1]})$. 
155	
156	Let $Z$ be an irreducible component of
157	$W_s$. 
158	%Let $j_s \colon \mathfrak{C}_s \rightarrow (\mathfrak{A}_g)_s$ be any Abel--Jacobi embedding.

166	Apply Lemma~\ref{LemmaPacketsAlternative3} to $A =

171	components of $W_s$. The number of irreducible components  is
172	bounded from above in an algebraic family.

175	$c_2$ to be such a number multiplied with $84(g-1)$. Thus

180	So we are not in case (i) of the proposition.

191	$\mathsection$\ref{subsec:univfamily}.

200	% $\IP_\IQbar^n$ equals the degree of $\mathfrak{C}_s$ and hence is bounded from above independently 
201	% of $s$.

204	 % for a particular $P_s$. But this sentence is easy to prove (B\'ezout
205	 % and/or flatness).} \Pcomment{Yes, we don't need
206	 % Lemma~\ref{lem:uniformdegreebound} and just need that the degree is

208	%\Pinlinecomment{New.}
209	Moreover, $W_s$ is Zariski closed in
210	$X_s\subset \cA_{s}$. Still holding $s$ fixed we may take $W_s$ as a Zariski
211	closed subset of $(\IP_{\IQbar}^n)^M$. Being the fiber above $s$ of a
212	subvariety of $(\IP_{\IQbar}^n)^M\times S^{\mathrm{sm}}$, we find that the degree
213	of $W_s$ is bounded from above independently of $s$.

215	Lemma~\ref{LemmaNAlon} we
216	thus obtain a number $c_4$, depending only  on these bounds and with the
217	following property. Any subset $\Sigma \subset
218	\mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$ with cardinality $\ge c_4$ satisfies $(\Sigma-P)^M
219	\not\subset W_s$.

227	% < c_1$ with $c_1=3c'/c$. Indeed,
